"Customer doesn't have enough points to deduct."Negative adjustment exceeds the member's current balanceDeduct no more than the Available balance shown on the row.
A member is missing from the listThey have never earned a point (lifetime 0 and balance 0)Expected — grant them a starter adjustment or wait for their first qualifying action. Use search to confirm the name/phone.
Redemption value dropped for everyone"Points per ₹1 redeemed" was increasedVerify the ratio is intentional; the ledger is unchanged but each point is now worth less wallet credit.

Frequently asked questions

Do customers have to sign up for Pure Rewards?

No. Every registered DOODLY customer is enrolled automatically. There is no separate opt-in; they simply start earning.

If I lower an earning rate, do customers lose points they already earned?

No. Rate changes apply only to future earns. Points already awarded keep their value and their original 12-month (or whatever expiryDays was at award time) expiry.

Can a customer's tier go down when they redeem?

No. Tiers are based on lifetime points earned, which never decreases. Redeeming and expiry reduce the available balance but never the tier.

Who can adjust a member's points, and is it tracked?

Only roles with loyalty:edit (Super Admin, Admin, Marketing by default). Every adjustment is written to the audit log as loyalty.adjust with the member, the signed amount and the reason you type.

What happens to points when an account is deleted?

Per the programme terms, if an account is permanently deleted all unused points expire. Points also cannot be transferred between accounts or cashed out — they are redeemable only as DOODLY Wallet credit.

✅
Best practice — change rates deliberately, not casually

Because these settings drive real money (redemptions become wallet credit) and every automatic earn across the platform, treat a change like a policy decision: adjust one thing at a time, note the business reason, Save, and confirm the audit entry. For temporary boosts prefer a time-boxed bonus campaign over editing base rates, so earning returns to normal automatically when the end date passes.

DOODLYAdmin · Growth · Customer Reviews
§Admin · Growth

Customer Reviews

See every product rating (1–5★) customers submit, moderate them one by one — approve, reject, hide, feature or reply — and rely on a hard server-side gate that lets only approved, verified, 5★ reviews ever appear on the public website, with every action written to the audit trail.

ℹ️
One page, one moderation module

The whole module lives at one screen (/admin/reviews.html, block type reviewsAdmin, mount #reviewsAdminMount, rendered by assets/js/reviews.js → DOODLY_REVIEWS.mountAdmin). It loads two calls in parallel — GET /api/admin/reviews?view=analytics and GET /api/admin/reviews?view=list — then paints a KPI strip and a single "All reviews" table with filters, search, per-row moderation buttons and a CSV export. Each part is documented below as its own screen.

✅
The public site is protected by policy, not by trust

Nothing you do in this module can accidentally leak an unfit review onto the storefront. The public feed (GET /api/reviews/public) hard-filters on the server to status = APPROVED and rating = 5 and a linked delivered order (orderId set = verified purchase). Pending, rejected, hidden, unverified, or 1–4★ reviews can never be shown publicly, no matter what. This module is for quality monitoring of everything; the storefront only ever sees your best, verified five-star feedback.

ℹ️
Where it sits in the menu

Although this manual groups Reviews under Growth, in the live admin sidebar the link appears under the Content section: Admin → Content → Reviews. The paths below use that real location.

Reviews dashboard — KPI strip

Platform-wide rating health at a glance, across every review (not just the public ones).

Purpose Show, at the top of the page, seven read-only totals computed across all reviews in the database — every rating, every status — so you can judge overall satisfaction and spot a run of low scores before you moderate a single row.
Access Super Admin Admin Support — anyone with support:view Path Admin → Content → Reviews
Header — Customer Reviews · "All ratings (1–5★) for quality monitoring — only APPROVED 5★ verified reviews appear on the public site."
Total reviewsCount of every review ever submitted
Average ratingMean of all ratings, shown as "X.X ★"
5★ reviewsHow many five-star ratings exist
4★ reviewsHow many four-star ratings exist
3★ reviewsHow many three-star ratings exist
2★ reviewsHow many two-star ratings exist
1★ reviewsHow many one-star ratings exist
Figure — the seven KPI cards, drawn from GET /api/admin/reviews?view=analytics
KPI cardWhat it countsHow it is calculated (backend)Format
Total reviewsEvery review on the platform, all statusesdb.review.count()Whole number (Indian grouping)
Average ratingOverall mean satisfaction across all reviews_avg.rating over every review, rounded to 2 decimals; the card prints one decimal + a star, e.g. "4.6 ★"Decimal + ★
5★ reviewsNumber of five-star ratingsgroupBy rating → the count where rating = 5Whole number
4★ reviewsNumber of four-star ratingscount where rating = 4Whole number
3★ reviewsNumber of three-star ratingscount where rating = 3Whole number
2★ reviewsNumber of two-star ratingscount where rating = 2Whole number
1★ reviewsNumber of one-star ratingscount where rating = 1Whole number
ℹ️
These cards are read-only and count everything

There are no buttons on the KPI strip. The counts include pending, rejected and hidden reviews too — this is a quality-monitoring view of the raw feedback, deliberately wider than what the public sees. The strip refreshes whenever the module (re)loads, which happens after every moderation action.

💡
More analytics exist in the response than the strip shows

The analytics endpoint also returns a per-product breakdown (byProduct: count and average per product), a 6-month trend (monthly: count and average per calendar month), and a reply count (replied). The current screen renders the totals and the 1–5 distribution; the extra fields are available in the API for reporting.

All reviews — list, filters & search

Every review in one table, filterable by status and rating, searchable, exportable.

Purpose Browse and narrow the full review list, read each customer's rating and words, see its moderation status, and act on it. This is the working surface of the module.
Access Read: Super Admin Admin Support (support:view). The per-row action buttons appear only with support:edit; a view-only role sees "view" in the Actions column instead. Path Admin → Content → Reviews → All reviews
Panel — All reviews · Status filter · Rating filter · Search box · Export CSV
Status filterAll statuses / Pending / Approved / Rejected / Hidden
Rating filterAll ratings / 5★ / 4★ / 3★ / 2★ / 1★
Search"Search name, email, review…" (debounced)
Export CSVDownloads the currently listed rows
Reviews tableCustomer · Rating · Review · Product · Status · Featured · Date · Actions
Figure — the All reviews panel: three filters, a search box, the export button and the data table

Table columns

ColumnWhat it showsBackend source
CustomerThe reviewer's name in bold, with their email (or phone, if no email) underneath. Shows "—" if the name is missing.user.name, user.email / user.phone
RatingThe star rating drawn as filled stars (1–5).rating (integer 1–5)
ReviewThe optional headline in bold (trimmed to 60 chars) then the comment text (trimmed to 140 chars). If a staff reply exists it shows on a second line as "↳ …" (trimmed to 120).title, comment, reply
ProductThe product reviewed — the order label, falling back to the product slug, then "—" (trimmed to 40 chars).target → productSlug
StatusA colour-coded badge: Pending (amber), Approved (green), Rejected (red), Hidden (grey).status
FeaturedA "★" when the review is featured (sorts first publicly), otherwise "—".featured
DateWhen the review was submitted, formatted as "12 Jul 2026".createdAt
ActionsModeration buttons (see Screen 3) with edit rights; the word "view" with read-only rights.—

Filters, search, sorting & export

AspectBehaviour (from the real code)
Status filterDropdown: All statuses (default) plus Pending, Approved, Rejected, Hidden. Changing it re-queries the server (&status=…, upper-cased) and repaints only the table rows.
Rating filterDropdown: All ratings (default) plus 5★, 4★, 3★, 2★, 1★. Changing it re-queries with &rating=….
SearchOne search box matching customer name, email, review comment or title (case-insensitive, server-side). Typing is debounced ~300 ms and re-queries with &q=…; only the rows repaint.
Filter persistenceThe chosen status, rating and search text are held in memory (_f) and survive a full module reload — so after you approve a row the list comes back showing the same filtered view.
SortingFixed: newest first (createdAt descending). There are no clickable column sorts.
PaginationNone. The list requests up to 200 rows (the server caps any request at 500). Narrow with the filters and search rather than paging.
ExportExport CSV downloads the rows currently listed (i.e. after your filters/search) as reviews-YYYY-MM-DD.csv with columns: Date, Customer, Email, Rating, Title, Comment, Product, Status, Featured, Reply. A toast confirms how many rows were exported.
Empty state"No reviews match." when nothing fits the current filters/search.
Offline / no access"Backend offline — the reviews admin needs the DOODLY API." if the API is unreachable; "You don't have permission to view customer reviews." if your role lacks support:view.
💡
Fast triage recipe

Set the Status filter to Pending and Rating to 5★ to see exactly the queue that could go public with one click — the approved-verified-5★ candidates. Then switch Rating to 1★/2★ to work the follow-up queue for unhappy customers. The Export button captures whatever slice you're looking at, so you can share a filtered batch with the team.

ℹ️
The server exposes more filters than the screen

The list endpoint also accepts productSlug and a from/to date range. The current screen wires up status, rating and free-text search; the extra parameters are available for API-driven reporting.

Moderate a review — row actions

Approve, reject, hide, feature/unfeature — the per-row buttons that decide what the public sees.

Purpose Change a review's moderation status or its featured flag. Only approved + verified + 5★ reviews reach the storefront, so approving is what publishes; rejecting/hiding removes; featuring pushes to the front of the public list.
Access Super Admin Admin Support — requires support:edit. A role with only support:view sees the review but no action buttons. Path Admin → Content → Reviews → All reviews → (row) Actions
Row actions — Approve · Reject · Hide · Feature / Unfeature · Reply
ApproveShown only when the review isn't already approved
RejectMarks the review rejected and un-features it
HideMarks the review hidden and un-features it
Feature / UnfeatureToggles the featured flag (label flips)
ReplyOpens the reply dialog (Screen 4)
Figure — the moderation buttons that appear in each row's Actions cell
ButtonWhat it doesBackend processPermission
ApprovePublishes the review to the moderation "approved" set. Only shown when the current status is not already Approved. Toast: "Review approved ✓".POST /api/admin/reviews {action:"approve"} → sets status = APPROVED, stamps moderatedBy/moderatedAt. If status changed, the product's public star average is recomputed. Audited review.approve.support:edit
RejectMarks the review rejected and clears its featured flag. Toast: "Review rejected".{action:"reject"} → status = REJECTED, featured = false, stamped. Product average recomputed if status changed. Audited review.reject.support:edit
HideMarks the review hidden and clears its featured flag — used when a review isn't outright rejected but shouldn't display. Toast: "Review hidden".{action:"hide"} → status = HIDDEN, featured = false, stamped. Product average recomputed if status changed. Audited review.hide.support:edit
Feature / UnfeatureToggles whether the review sorts first on the public site. The button label reflects the current state — "Feature" when it isn't, "Unfeature" when it is. Toasts: "Marked as featured ★" / "Removed from featured".{action:"feature"} / {action:"unfeature"} → sets featured true/false, stamped. Status is unchanged, so the product average is not recomputed. Audited review.feature / review.unfeature.support:edit
ReplyOpens the reply modal to write a message shown to the customer (see Screen 4).No write until you send from the modal.support:edit
Workflow — moderating one review
Filter to PendingRead the rating & textApprove / Reject / HideOptionally FeatureServer stamps moderator + recomputes product averageAction auditedModule reloads, KPIs & rows refresh
Figure — end-to-end path of a single moderation decision
⚠️
Approving a 1–4★ review does not put its text on the storefront — but it does move the star average

The public testimonial cards only ever show 5★ reviews. However, the numeric product rating and 1–5 distribution shown on a product page are computed over all approved, verified reviews of any rating. So if you approve a verified 3★ review, that product's displayed average will drop accordingly (the stored Product.ratingValue/ratingCount are recomputed automatically). Approve honestly — approval is what makes a rating "count" toward the public score, even when the words stay private.

ℹ️
Every action is stamped and audited

Each moderation writes a moderator stamp — the acting role and user id (moderatedBy) plus a timestamp (moderatedAt) — onto the review, and appends an audit record such as review.approve whose target reads "<id> (PENDING → APPROVED)". You can always see who changed a review, from which status to which, and when, in Admin → System → Audit Logs.

💡
Reject vs. Hide

Both remove a review from public display and un-feature it. Use Reject for feedback you've decided is not acceptable (spam, abuse, off-topic). Use Hide to temporarily pull down a genuine review (for example while you follow up with the customer) with the intent to reconsider. The distinction is captured in the audit and lets you filter the two apart later.

ℹ️
Nothing here is permanently deleted

There is no delete button. Rejecting or hiding only changes a review's status; the record — and the customer's original words — is kept. This is deliberate: feedback is never destroyed, so a decision can always be reviewed or reversed by approving the review again.

Reply to a review (modal)

Write a response the customer will see against their review.

Purpose Thank a happy customer or respond to a concern. The reply is stored on the review and is shown to the customer; it also appears as the "↳ …" line under the review in this table.
Access Super Admin Admin Support — requires support:edit Path Admin → Content → Reviews → All reviews → (row) Reply
Modal — Reply to review — {customer name}
Original reviewThe customer's comment quoted for context (up to 200 chars)
ReplyTextarea "Reply shown to the customer" (up to 1000 chars), pre-filled with any existing reply
MessageInline validation / error line
Cancel / Send replyGhost + primary buttons
Figure — the reply dialog opened from a row's Reply button
FieldPurposeAccepted valuesReq.Validation / backendExample
ReplyThe message shown to the customer against their reviewText, 1–1000 charactersYesClient rejects an empty reply ("Write a reply first."). Server (Zod) trims and enforces 1–1000 chars; stores it on reply and sets repliedAt.Thank you so much, Priya — glad you loved the creaminess! 🥛
ButtonWhat it doesBackend processPermission
Send replySaves the reply; shows "Sending…", then closes and reloads the module. Toast: "Reply saved ✓".POST /api/admin/reviews {action:"reply", reviewId, reply} → sets reply + repliedAt, stamps the moderator. Audited review.reply. Status is unchanged (a reply doesn't publish a review).support:edit
CancelCloses the dialog without saving. Clicking the dark overlay also closes it.None.support:view
ℹ️
Editing an existing reply

Opening Reply on a review that already has a response pre-fills the box with the current text, so sending again overwrites it. There's no separate "delete reply" button; to remove a reply, you'd replace it with new text.

✅
A reply is customer-facing — write it like a public statement

Because the reply is shown to the customer (and travels with the review), keep it warm, specific and on-brand. For a complaint, acknowledge the issue and say what you'll do — don't argue in the reply. Pair a reply with the right status decision (Hide while you resolve, then Approve if it's a genuine 5★).

How a review is born (reference)

The customer-side flow that fills this queue — for context, not a screen you operate.

Purpose Understand where the reviews you moderate come from, why they're all "verified", and why some of them ping you with an alert — so your moderation decisions make sense end to end.
Access Super Admin reference Path Customer → My Orders → Rate (source), then Admin → Content → Reviews
Workflow — review lifecycle
Customer's order is PAID & DELIVEREDThey write a rating + words from My OrdersServer verifies ownership & delivery → saves as PENDINGLoyalty points awarded once per order1–2★ alerts admins/supportYou moderate here (approve / reject / hide / feature / reply)Approved + verified + 5★ → public site
Figure — from a delivered order to a published (or moderated) review
FactDetail (from /api/account/reviews)
Who can reviewOnly the customer who owns a paid, not-cancelled, delivered order. The server never trusts the client — it re-checks ownership, payment and delivery before saving.
Why "verified"Each review is linked to the specific delivered order (orderId), which is unique — one review per order — so every review here is a verified purchase.
One per productBuying the same product again does not grant a second public review slot; the customer edits their existing one instead.
What they submitA rating (1–5, required), an optional title (≤120 chars) and an optional comment (≤1000 chars). It's saved as PENDING — nothing is public until you approve.
Loyalty tie-inSubmitting a verified review awards DOODLY Pure Rewards points once per delivered order (idempotent), per the loyalty config's "Verified review" rate.
Low-rating alertA 1★ or 2★ review sends an in-app notification to active Admins, Super Admins and Support: "⚠️ X★ review needs follow-up … review it in Admin → Reviews." The feedback is never auto-deleted.
Customer editsIf a customer edits their own review, it is reset to PENDING, un-featured, its moderation stamp cleared, and the product's public average recomputed — so an edited review re-enters your queue and never stays public un-reviewed.
⚠️
An approved review can quietly go back to Pending

Because a customer editing their review resets it to Pending, a review you approved yesterday can reappear in the Pending queue today if they changed it. Re-moderate edited reviews; the audit shows the customer's review.edit event.

ℹ️
Where approved 5★ reviews surface publicly

The homepage "What DOODLY families say" grid and each product page's "Ratings & Reviews" section read GET /api/reviews/public. Names are anonymised (first name + last initial, e.g. "Vivek D."), featured reviews sort first, then newest. If there are none yet, the site shows an honest empty state — never demo data.

Troubleshooting

SymptomLikely causeFix
"You don't have permission to view customer reviews."Signed-in role lacks support:viewSign in as Super Admin, Admin or Support, or grant the role Customer Support access in Admin → System → Roles & Permissions.
The table shows reviews but no action buttons — just "view"Role has support:view but not support:editExpected read-only behaviour. Use an edit-capable role (Super Admin, Admin, Support) to moderate.
"Backend offline — the reviews admin needs the DOODLY API."The Next.js API isn't reachable from the storefrontConfirm the backend is running and the API base URL is correct; reload the page.
An approved 5★ review isn't showing on the websiteIt isn't verified (no linked delivered order), or it's not featured and lower down the newest list, or cachingOnly approved + verified (order-linked) + 5★ reviews publish. Check the Product column links a real order; consider Feature to pull it to the top; reload the storefront.
A product's average rating dropped after moderationYou approved a verified 1–4★ review, which now counts toward the public averageExpected — the average is computed over all approved verified reviews. If unintended, Hide or Reject the review to recompute it back out.
"Review not found." when acting on a rowThe review was changed/removed in another tab, or a stale pageReload the module so the list re-queries, then retry.
Nothing exports / CSV is emptyThe current filters/search match no rowsExport uses the rows currently listed. Clear the filters or broaden the search, then Export CSV.
A previously approved review is back in PendingThe customer edited their own review, which resets it to PendingRe-moderate it. The review.edit entry in Audit Logs confirms the customer changed it.

Frequently asked questions

Why do I see 1–4★ reviews here if only 5★ ever go public?

This module is for quality monitoring of all feedback, not just the public showcase. You need to read every rating to spot problems, follow up on unhappy customers and keep the product average honest. The public feed applies its own hard filter (approved + verified + 5★) on top.

Can I permanently delete a review?

No. There is no delete. You can Reject or Hide it (which removes it from public view and un-features it), but the record and the customer's words are always kept, so any decision can be reversed by approving again.

Does replying to a review make it public?

No. A reply is saved and shown to the customer, but it does not change the review's status. Publishing still requires the review to be Approved, verified and 5★.

Who gets notified about a bad review?

When a customer submits a 1★ or 2★ review, all active Admins, Super Admins and Support staff receive an in-app "needs follow-up" notification pointing back to this module. The review itself stays untouched until you moderate it.

Which roles can moderate reviews?

By default Super Admin, Admin and Support (Customer Support) — the roles that hold the support permission. Viewing needs support:view; approving, rejecting, hiding, featuring and replying need support:edit. You can adjust these in Admin → System → Roles & Permissions.

How are customer names shown on the website?

Anonymised. The public feed shows only the first name and the last initial (for example "Vivek D."), never the full name, email or phone. Full identity is visible only inside this internal module.

✅
Best practice — a daily review routine

Once a day, open Admin → Content → Reviews and: (1) filter to 1★/2★ and reply to or resolve each, hiding while you follow up; (2) filter to Pending + 5★ and approve genuine ones, featuring the standout stories; (3) reject spam or abuse. Because everything is audited and nothing is deleted, you can always show exactly how each piece of feedback was handled.

Module chapter

Admin · Content

5 screen groups documented from the live application.

DOODLYAdmin · Content · Blogs
§Admin · Content

Blogs

The DOODLY journal manager — write, schedule and publish the articles that appear on the customer website, with a full Draft → Scheduled → Published ⇄ Archived lifecycle, automatic slug and reading-time, SEO metadata, live view counts, soft-delete/restore, and a one-click preview, all backed by the DOODLY database.

ℹ️
What this screen is, and how it is built

The Blogs item under Admin → Content opens the storefront admin page /admin/blogs.html. That page renders a single block, blogBoard, which mounts into #blogMount and is driven entirely by the live DOODLY database through the API /api/admin/blog. Everything you see — the seven KPI cards, the search box and two dropdowns, the posts table, the New post and Manage forms — is real data. When the backend is reachable a green “● Live” banner confirms it and names the database (for example “● Live — 12 post(s) · 8 published · 3 draft · 1 scheduled · 4,120 views from the DOODLY database”). Published posts (and scheduled posts whose time has arrived) appear on the public customer journal, ordered newest-first, and each page-view they receive is counted back into the Views column here.

ℹ️
Who can view and who can manage

Two permission levels guard this module. Viewing (the board, KPIs and table) is open to any role that can view blogs, reports or CMS — that is Super Admin, Admin and Marketing directly, plus read-only report viewers such as Accountant, Operations, Procurement, Quality and Inventory (through their reports:view access). Managing a post — create, edit, publish/unpublish, duplicate, delete/restore — requires the blogs edit permission, which by default only Super Admin, Admin and Marketing hold. A blocked mutation returns HTTP 403 and the screen shows “Only Marketing / Admin can manage the blog (403)”. The server-side checks canViewBlog / canManageBlog are the real boundary — hidden or greyed buttons are convenience only.

ℹ️
Status is partly derived — you set a state, the system decides the badge

You choose a post’s state (Draft / Published / Scheduled / Archived) in the form, but the coloured status badge on the table is computed from that state together with the schedule and the deletion flag. The rule, in order: deleted → Deleted (red); state ARCHIVED → Archived (grey); state DRAFT → Draft (amber); state SCHEDULED with a future schedule date → Scheduled (blue); state SCHEDULED whose date has already passed → Published (green); anything else → Published. So a post set to Scheduled for next Monday shows as Scheduled until that day and then goes live — and starts appearing on the customer website — on its own, with no manual toggling.

ℹ️
Slug and reading-time are automatic

You never have to calculate two of the fields. The slug (the URL-friendly part of the address, e.g. why-a2-milk) is generated from the title if you leave it blank — lower-cased, spaces and symbols turned to dashes, & turned to “and”, trimmed to 80 characters — and is always made unique by appending -2, -3 and so on if that slug already exists. The reading time is recomputed every time you save from the article body: HTML tags are stripped, words are counted, divided by 200 words-per-minute, and rounded up to at least 1 minute. It shows as “N min read” under the title and in the editor header.

Blog Board

Seven live KPI cards over a searchable, filterable, sortable table of every post — the editorial pulse of the journal at a glance.

Purpose Show at a glance how many posts exist and in what state, how many total views the journal has earned and which post is winning — then let you drill into any one post to manage it, or start a new one.
Access Super Admin Admin Marketing (+ read-only report/CMS viewers) — view. Managing requires the blogs edit permission. Path Admin → Content → Blogs
Blogs — Board · header “New post” button · 7 KPI cards · toolbar · table
Page headerTitle “Blog Manager” + intro line + New post primary button (top-right)
KPI row (7)Total Posts · Published · Drafts · Scheduled · Archived · Total Views · Top Post
ToolbarSearch box · status dropdown (“All statuses”) · sort dropdown (“Newest”)
Posts tablePanel headed “Posts” with a live count badge; columns Post · Author · Category · Views · Status · Updated · Manage
Live bannerGreen “● Live — N post(s) · M published · K draft · S scheduled · V views” from the database, or an amber/red offline / 403 notice
Figure — the Blog board: KPI cards, a three-control toolbar, and the posts table.

Every KPI card and where the number comes from

KPI cardPurposeAccepted valuesReq.Validation / backendExample
Total PostsEvery live (non-deleted) postCountRead-onlyCount of all posts where deletedAt is null (kpis.totalPosts).12
PublishedPosts live on the website nowCountRead-onlyPosts whose derived status is Published — includes scheduled posts whose date has passed (kpis.published).8
DraftsWritten but not launchedCountRead-onlyPosts in state DRAFT (kpis.drafts).3
ScheduledSet to publish laterCountRead-onlyPosts in state SCHEDULED whose date is still in the future (kpis.scheduled).1
ArchivedRetired from the journalCountRead-onlyPosts in state ARCHIVED (kpis.archived).0
Total ViewsAll page-views across the journalNumberRead-onlySum of the views counter over every non-deleted post (kpis.totalViews).4,120
Top PostThe most-read articlePost title (or “—”)Read-onlyThe non-deleted post with the highest views (kpis.topPost); its view count is also computed (topPostViews).Why A2 milk?

The toolbar — search and two dropdowns

ControlPurposeAccepted valuesReq.Validation / backendExample
Search boxFree-text findAny textOpt.Debounced ~300 ms; the API matches the post title, slug, excerpt, author and category, case-insensitive (q=). Placeholder reads “Search title, author, category…”.a2 milk
Status dropdownFilter by derived statusAll statuses · Published · Draft · Scheduled · ArchivedOpt.Filters the list to the chosen derived status label, matched case-insensitively (status=).Published
Sort dropdownOrder the listNewest · Recently updated · Recently published · Most viewed · Title A–ZOpt.Maps to sort= values updated / published / views / title; blank (“Newest”) sorts by created date, newest first.Most viewed

The posts table

ColumnShowsNotes
PostThe title in bold, with the category and the reading time (“N min read”) as small muted sub-lines beneath itThree stacked lines in one cell.
AuthorThe by-line, or “—” if none was setFree-text author name.
CategoryThe category name, or “—”Free-text category (no fixed list).
ViewsThe live page-view counter for that postIncremented by the public website each time the article is opened.
StatusColoured badge — Published (green), Scheduled (blue), Draft (amber), Archived (grey), Deleted (red)Derived, not stored (see the callout above).
UpdatedThe date the post was last changed (YYYY-MM-DD)From the record’s updatedAt.
ManageA Manage link that opens the Edit-post formOnly actionable for roles with manage permission.
Table behaviourHow it works
SearchLive debounced search over title / slug / excerpt / author / category.
FiltersStatus (All / Published / Draft / Scheduled / Archived), applied to the derived status label.
SortingNewest first by default; or recently updated, recently published, most viewed, or title A–Z.
PaginationThe board requests up to 500 rows at once (view=list&pageSize=500); the API paginates and reports total / page / pages.
Row actionManage → opens the Edit-post modal for that post.
Count badgeThe panel header shows the total number of matching posts.
Empty state“No posts match these filters.” when nothing matches.
💡
Tip — the search box does more than its placeholder promises

The placeholder says “title, author, category”, but the backend also searches the slug and the excerpt. So you can find a post by a phrase from its summary card, or by a fragment of its URL, not just by its headline.

New / Edit Post

The single modal form that writes a post or edits an existing one — title, slug, body, imagery, category, tags, status, schedule and SEO — plus Preview, Publish/Unpublish, Duplicate and Delete.

Purpose Capture everything about one article in one dialog: what it is called and where it lives (title, slug), what it says (excerpt, content), how it looks and is grouped (image, category, author, tags), when and whether it is live (status, schedule), and how search engines see it (SEO) — then save it to the database.
Access Super Admin Admin Marketing — the blogs edit permission is required; other roles get a 403. Path Admin → Content → Blogs → New post / Manage
Blogs — New / Edit post · modal dialog · fields + collapsible SEO · footer actions
HeaderTitle “New post”, or “Edit post · N views · M min” when editing + close (×)
Row 1Title* · Slug (auto if blank)
Row 2Category · Author
ExcerptCard summary (textarea)
ContentArticle body, HTML supported (monospace textarea)
Row 3Featured image URL · Tags (comma)
Row 4Status · Schedule for (if Scheduled)
SEO metadataCollapsible: SEO title · SEO description · SEO keywords (comma)
FooterPreview · Publish/Unpublish · Duplicate · Delete/Restore (edit only) · Cancel · Create post / Save changes
Figure — the post editor: the core fields, a full-width content area, a collapsible SEO section, and lifecycle actions in the footer.

Every field on the form

FieldPurposeAccepted valuesReq.Validation / backendExample
TitleThe headline of the articleTextYesAt least 3 characters (blocked client-side with “Enter a title (at least 3 characters).”); server enforces 3–200 chars (title).Why A2 milk is easier to digest
SlugThe URL-friendly address; blank = generated from the titleText (url-friendly)Opt.Placeholder “url-friendly-slug”. If blank, generated from the title by slugify; either way it is trimmed to 80 chars and made unique (adds -2, -3…). On edit, only changes if you type a new one. Field accepts up to 90 chars (slug).why-a2-milk
CategoryGroups the post on the journalFree textOpt.Any free-text name (no fixed list), up to 80 chars; blank stored as none (categoryName). Placeholder “Health”.Health
AuthorThe by-line shown on the articleFree textOpt.Up to 120 chars; blank stored as none (author). Placeholder “Team DOODLY”.Team DOODLY
ExcerptThe short summary shown on cards and previewsTextOpt.Up to 400 chars (excerpt); rendered in italics on the preview and used as the card teaser.A2 milk carries only the A2 beta-casein protein…
ContentThe full article bodyHTML / textOpt.Up to 100,000 chars; HTML is supported and rendered as-is (monospace editor). The reading time is recomputed from this on every save (content → readingMinutes).<p>Buffalo A2 milk…</p>
Featured image URLThe hero image at the top of the post and on the cardURLOpt.Up to 2,000 chars; stored as-is and shown full-width in the preview (featuredImage). Placeholder “https://…”.https://cdn.doodly.in/a2.jpg
TagsKeywords for grouping / related contentComma-separated wordsOpt.Split on commas, trimmed, blanks dropped → an array of up to 20 tags, each up to 40 chars (tags). Placeholder “a2, health”.a2, health, digestion
StatusThe lifecycle state of the postDraft · Published · Scheduled · ArchivedOpt.Stored as status; defaults to Draft on a new post. Choosing Published stamps publishedAt and puts it on the website; Scheduled needs a date (below).Published
Schedule forThe date a Scheduled post should go liveDateOpt.*A date picker (scheduledFor). Required when Status is Scheduled — otherwise saving is blocked with “Pick a schedule date for a scheduled post.” Only sent to the server when Status is Scheduled; cleared otherwise.2026-07-20
SEO titleThe <title> / search-result headlineTextOpt.Inside the collapsible SEO metadata section; up to 160 chars (seoTitle).A2 Milk & Digestion | DOODLY
SEO descriptionThe search-result snippetTextOpt.Up to 320 chars (seoDescription).Learn why A2 buffalo milk is gentler on the gut…
SEO keywordsKeyword hints for search enginesComma-separated wordsOpt.Split on commas → an array of up to 20 keywords, each up to 60 chars (seoKeywords).a2 milk, buffalo milk, digestion
ℹ️
The editor header shows read-only stats when editing

When you open an existing post, the modal title reads “Edit post · N views · M min” — the live view count and the current reading time. These are informational; you cannot type them. Views are driven by real customer traffic, and the reading time is recalculated from the Content field every time you save.

Buttons on the form and table

ButtonWhat it doesBackend processPermission
New post (header)Opens the blank post form (Status defaults to Draft)None until you save.Super Admin+ Admin Marketing
Manage (table row)Opens the Edit-post form pre-filled from that postNone until you save.Super Admin+ Admin Marketing
Create postValidates then saves a new postPOST action create → parses the schema, generates a unique slug, computes reading time, stamps publishedAt if Published, writes the post, appends an audit line, toasts “Post created”, reloads the board.Super Admin+ Admin Marketing
Save changesSaves edits to an existing postPOST action update → partial-schema update (incl. changing Status), regenerates the slug only if you changed it, recomputes reading time when Content changed, audits, toasts “Post updated”, reloads.Super Admin+ Admin Marketing
Preview (edit only)Opens the post in a new browser tab as a reader would see itLocal only — renders the current form values (image, category pill, title, meta line, excerpt, content) in a pop-up window with a “DOODLY preview — not the live page” banner. Needs pop-ups allowed.Super Admin+ Admin Marketing
Publish / Unpublish (edit only)Toggles the post live or back to Draft in one clickPOST action publish (sets status PUBLISHED + stamps publishedAt, toasts “Published”) or unpublish (sets status DRAFT, toasts “Unpublished”). The button label flips based on the current status.Super Admin+ Admin Marketing
Duplicate (edit only)Clones the post as a fresh DraftPOST action duplicate → copies all fields, names it “… (copy)”, gives it a “…-copy” slug (de-duplicated), resets views to 0 and status to DRAFT, clears publishedAt; toasts “Duplicated (draft)”.Super Admin+ Admin Marketing
Delete (edit only)Soft-deletes the post after a confirm promptPOST action delete → sets deletedAt (the post is hidden and pulled from the site, not erased). Confirms “Soft-delete post ‘…’?” first; toasts “Post deleted”.Super Admin+ Admin Marketing
Restore (edit only)Brings a deleted post backPOST action restore → clears deletedAt; toasts “Restored”. This button replaces Delete once a post is deleted.Super Admin+ Admin Marketing
Cancel / × / Esc / click-outsideCloses the form without savingNone.Anyone with the form open
💡
Tip — more lifecycle actions exist on the API than on the form

The form covers everyday editing, but the backend also exposes dedicated schedule and archive actions and a bulk action that can publish, unpublish, archive, delete or restore up to 1,000 posts in one call — available to the API and to any future multi-select tooling. On this screen you achieve the same outcomes through the Status dropdown plus the Publish / Delete buttons.

✅
Best Practice — preview before you publish, and fill the SEO fields

Because the Content field renders raw HTML, always click Preview to confirm the layout, image and formatting look right before you Publish. And spend the extra minute on the collapsible SEO metadata — an SEO title, a description and a handful of keywords make the article far easier to find on search engines than the auto-derived title alone.

Post Preview

A reader’s-eye rendering of the post, opened in a separate browser tab straight from the editor.

Purpose Let you see exactly how the article will look — hero image, category, headline, by-line, reading time, summary and body — before it goes live, using whatever you have currently typed in the form.
Access Super Admin Admin Marketing — from the Preview button on an existing post. Path Admin → Content → Blogs → Manage → Preview
Blogs — Preview · new browser tab · magazine-style article
Status bannerFixed top bar: “DOODLY preview — status: … — not the live page”
Featured imageFull-width hero (only if a Featured image URL is set)
Category pillThe category name in a rounded pill (if set)
HeadlineThe post title as a large heading
Meta lineCategory · Author · “N min read”
BodyThe excerpt in italics, then the article HTML rendered as the reader will see it
Figure — the preview tab: a serif, reading-optimised layout with a clear “not the live page” banner.
⚠️
Warning — allow pop-ups, and remember it uses unsaved values

Preview opens a new browser tab. If your browser blocks it you will see the toast “Allow pop-ups to preview.” — permit pop-ups for the admin site and try again. The preview reflects the current, possibly unsaved contents of the form, so it is a true what-you-see-is-what-you-get check — but nothing is written to the database until you click Save changes.

Lifecycle & publishing workflows

How a post moves from a blank draft to a live article — and to the customer website.

Purpose Show the two processes this module drives: the editorial lifecycle of one post, and how a published or due-scheduled post reaches the public journal and starts counting views.
Workflow — post lifecycle
New post (Draft)Write & PreviewPublish now → Publishedor Schedule → Scheduled → Published (on date)Unpublish ⇄ RepublishArchiveDelete → Restore
Figure — a post starts as a Draft; Publish (or a Scheduled date arriving) makes it live; it can be Unpublished back to Draft, Archived, or soft-Deleted and later Restored.
Workflow — how a post reaches the customer website
Status = Published (or Scheduled date passed)Public feed serves it, newest firstCustomer opens the articleView counter incrementsViews & Total Views update on the board
Figure — the public journal shows published posts (plus scheduled posts whose time has come), limited to the most recent set; every page-view is counted back into the admin board.
ℹ️
Scheduling is “publish on arrival”, not a background job

A Scheduled post does not need a cron to go live. The moment its Schedule for date has passed, the derived status becomes Published and the public feed starts serving it automatically (the feed asks for PUBLISHED posts and SCHEDULED posts whose date is on/before now). Until then the badge stays Scheduled (blue) and the article is invisible to customers.

✅
Best Practice — draft, preview, schedule, then let it launch itself

Write the post as a Draft, use Preview to check it renders correctly, fill in the SEO fields, then set Status to Scheduled with a future date and Save changes. It will publish on its own on that day — no need to remember to come back and flip the switch. Reserve the instant Publish button for time-sensitive news.

⛔
Caution — delete is reversible, but the post disappears from the site immediately

Deleting is a soft delete: the post and its view history are kept and can be Restored. But the instant you delete, the post is pulled from the public journal and hidden from the default board. If you only want to take an article off the site temporarily, prefer Unpublish or Archive over Delete so it stays visible in your working list.

Troubleshooting & FAQ

Common issues and answers.

Troubleshooting

SymptomLikely causeFix
Amber/red banner instead of the “● Live” one; no posts loadThe Next.js backend is offline, or your role can’t view the blogStart the backend; if it reads “403”, ask an Admin to grant blogs / reports / CMS view access.
“Only Marketing / Admin can manage the blog (403).”Your role has view but not edit on the blogs moduleHave a Super Admin, Admin or Marketing user make the change, or request the permission.
Saving is blocked with “Enter a title (at least 3 characters).”The Title is empty or under 3 charactersType a title of at least 3 characters.
Saving is blocked with “Pick a schedule date for a scheduled post.”Status is Scheduled but the Schedule-for date is emptyPick a date, or change the Status away from Scheduled.
“Allow pop-ups to preview.”The browser blocked the preview tabAllow pop-ups for the admin site, then click Preview again.
Post shows Scheduled, customers can’t see it yetIts Schedule-for date is still in the futureExpected — it will publish itself on that date, or Publish it now to go live immediately.
Two posts have almost the same URL (…-2)The slug you chose already existed, so a number was appended for uniquenessEdit the Slug field to a distinct value if you want a cleaner URL.
Deleted a post by mistake and it vanished from the listSoft delete hides it from the default boardThe record is safe; use the API’s includeDeleted view (or ask an admin) to find it and Restore.

Frequently asked questions

Q: What is the difference between Unpublish, Archive and Delete?

Unpublish sends a live post back to Draft so you can keep editing it; it leaves the website. Archive retires a post (state Archived, grey badge) — a tidy “done with this” state that keeps it in the list. Delete soft-deletes it: hidden from the site and the default board, but recoverable via Restore. All three take the article off the public journal; they differ in intent and where the post then lives in your workflow.

Q: Do I have to write the slug and reading time myself?

No. Leave the Slug blank and it is generated from the title (and made unique automatically). The reading time is always calculated from the Content on save — you never type it.

Q: My post is set to Published but shows the Scheduled badge — why?

That happens when the Status is actually Scheduled with a future date, not Published. The blue Scheduled badge means “not live yet”. It flips to the green Published badge automatically once the schedule date passes, or immediately if you click Publish or clear the schedule.

Q: Can I use images and formatting in the article?

Yes. The Content field supports HTML, and there is a dedicated Featured image URL for the hero image. Because raw HTML is rendered, always Preview before publishing to confirm it looks right.

Q: Where do published posts actually appear?

On the public DOODLY customer website’s journal. The public feed serves posts whose derived status is Published (including scheduled posts whose date has arrived), newest first. Each time a reader opens an article its Views counter goes up, which feeds the Views column and the Total Views KPI here.

Q: Does duplicating a post copy its views and publish it?

No. A duplicate is created as a fresh Draft named “… (copy)” with a new “…-copy” slug, its view count reset to zero and no publish date. It never touches the original — it is a safe starting point for a similar article.

DOODLYAdmin · Content · CMS
§Admin · Content

CMS

The Content Management System — one place to edit the words, images, banners, FAQs, legal pages, testimonials and product details that appear on the public DOODLY storefront, so marketing copy and product launches can change without a code deploy.

ℹ️
Where this lives & what it really is

Open it from Admin → Content → CMS (URL /admin/cms.html). The page has two halves. The top half is a hub of six tiles that route you to each content area. The bottom half is the Content Blocks manager — a live table of every editable content block stored in the DOODLY database, driven through the endpoints /api/admin/cms (list & create) and /api/admin/cms/<id> (edit & delete). When it loads you will see a green banner reading “● Live — N content block(s) · P published from the DOODLY database”. If the banner is red, the backend is offline or your role is not allowed — see Troubleshooting.

ℹ️
Two content systems, one screen

CMS covers two kinds of content that are edited from different places but both reach the live site: (1) Content blocks — free-form JSON records of type page, banner, testimonial, legal, hero, faq and so on, stored in the CmsBlock table, published blocks are overlaid onto storefront pages at load time; and (2) Product content — every attribute of a product (pricing, variants, plans, nutrition, quality, description, badges, images, availability, SEO), edited through the tabbed Product CMS editor that opens from the Products screen. Both are documented below.

CMS Hub

The landing screen — six shortcut tiles that jump to each content area.

Purpose Give a plain-English map of everything that can be edited on the public site, and one-click routes into each area.
Access Super Admin Admin can view, create, edit and delete; Marketing can view, create and edit but not delete. No other role can open this page. Path Admin → Content → CMS
Header — CMS · “Edit storefront content — pages, FAQs, banners, products.”
Tile 1 · Products & status“Launch products with a status flip.” → opens /admin/products.html (link: Open)
Tile 2 · FAQs“Edit the customer FAQ.” → routes to the Help Center admin
Tile 3 · Pages“About, farmers, quality copy.” → opens the page-type block manager
Tile 4 · Banners“Homepage hero & promos.” → opens the banner-type block manager
Tile 5 · Testimonials“Manage reviews shown publicly.” → opens the testimonial-type block manager
Tile 6 · Legal“Privacy, terms, refund, shipping.” → opens the legal-type block manager
Live status banner + Content Blocks managerBelow the tiles — see Screen 2
Figure — the CMS hub: six routing tiles on top, the live Content Blocks manager below.

What each tile does

Each tile shows an icon, a title and a one-line description. When the page loads, the five non-product tiles gain a green “Manage →” hint and become clickable buttons (keyboard-accessible with Enter or Space). Here is exactly where each one takes you.

TileIconWhat it saysWhat clicking it does
Products & statustag“Launch products with a status flip.”A real link — opens /admin/products.html, where each product's Edit button launches the tabbed Product CMS editor (Screen 4). This is how you flip a product between Draft, Coming Soon and Active.
FAQsmsg“Edit the customer FAQ.”Navigates to the live Help Center admin at /admin/help-center.html, where FAQ questions and answers are managed.
Pagesedit“About, farmers, quality copy.”Opens the typed block manager filtered to blocks of type page (About, Farmers, Quality prose sections).
Bannersgift“Homepage hero & promos.”Opens the typed block manager filtered to blocks of type banner (homepage hero & promo strips).
Testimonialsstar“Manage reviews shown publicly.”Opens the typed block manager filtered to blocks of type testimonial.
Legalfile“Privacy, terms, refund, shipping.”Opens the typed block manager filtered to blocks of type legal.
💡
Tip — the tiles are shortcuts, not separate stores

Pages, Banners, Testimonials and Legal all point at the same Content Blocks table underneath — the tiles simply pre-filter it to one type and pre-fill the Key with the right prefix when you create a new block. You can achieve the same thing from the Content Blocks manager below by choosing the type yourself.

Content Blocks Manager

The live table of every editable content block, with headline counts on top.

Purpose See, create, edit and remove every free-form content block that the storefront reads — hero copy, banners, page prose, testimonials and legal text — all backed by the DOODLY database.
Access View/Create/Edit — Super Admin Admin Marketing. Delete — Super Admin Admin only. Path Admin → Content → CMS → (scroll to Content blocks)
Panel — Content blocks · [＋ New block]
KPI strip (4 cards)Total Blocks · Published · Drafts · Types
Live status bannerGreen “● Live — N content block(s) · P published…”; red if offline or blocked
Blocks tableColumns: Key · Type · Status · Updated · Manage
Row action“Manage” opens the block editor dialog
Figure — the Content Blocks manager: four KPI cards, then a table of every block.

The four dashboard cards (KPI strip)

These are computed live from the list of blocks returned by /api/admin/cms, and sit above the table.

CardWhat it counts
Total BlocksEvery content block in the database, published or not.
PublishedBlocks whose Published flag is on — the ones the live site actually reads.
DraftsTotal minus Published — blocks saved but not yet live.
TypesHow many distinct block types exist (e.g. page, banner, testimonial, legal, hero).

The content-blocks table

Every block is one row. The table is sorted by Key (A→Z) — the order returned by the backend.

ElementDetail (from the real render)
ColumnsKey (the unique block identifier, e.g. hero.home or page.about, shown in bold) · Type (the block type, e.g. banner) · Status (a green “Published” badge or a grey “Draft” badge) · Updated (the last-changed date, shown as YYYY-MM-DD, or “—” if never) · Manage (opens the editor).
SortingFixed — ascending by Key, straight from the database.
Empty stateWhen there are no blocks yet, the table shows “No content blocks yet — create one.”
Row actionManage opens the block editor dialog (next section) pre-filled with that block.
CreateThe panel header carries a ＋ New block button that opens the same dialog empty.
ℹ️
Note — this table has no search, filter or export

Unlike the big operational tables elsewhere in the admin, the Content Blocks list is a simple database-ordered table without the DataTable toolbar. To narrow to one type, use the hub tiles (Pages / Banners / Testimonials / Legal), which open a per-type list instead.

Create / Edit block dialog

The ＋ New block button and every Manage button open the same modal form. It has four inputs.

FieldPurposeAccepted valuesReq.Validation / backendExample
KeyThe unique name the storefront uses to find this block. Letters, numbers, dot, dash, underscore.Yes Client & server both enforce ^[a-z0-9._-]+$, length 2–60. Must be unique — a duplicate key returns a “conflict” error (“A block with that key already exists.”). Locked (disabled) when editing an existing block — a key can never be renamed. hero.home
TypeGroups the block and decides which storefront slot reads it. A short label — hero, banner, faq, page, testimonial, legal…Yes Free text, length 2–30. The hub tiles preset this (page / banner / testimonial / legal); you may set any type from the generic form. banner
Data (JSON payload)The actual content — a flexible JSON object holding the fields for that block type. Any valid JSON object.Opt. Must parse as valid JSON or the form refuses to save (“Data must be valid JSON.”). Empty defaults to {}. Stored verbatim in the CmsBlock.data column. Common keys read by pages: eyebrow, heading, text, html. { "heading": "Fresh A2 milk", "text": "Delivered by 7 AM" }
PublishedWhether this block is live on the public site. On / Off checkbox.Opt. On by default for new blocks. Only published blocks are returned to the storefront by /api/cms/page; unpublished ones are saved as drafts and stay invisible to customers. On

Buttons in the block dialog

ButtonWhat it doesBackend processPermission
Create block (new mode)Saves a brand-new block and refreshes the table.POST /api/admin/cms with key, type, data, published. Records a cms.create audit entry.Super Admin Admin Marketing
Save changes (edit mode)Updates the block's type, data and/or published flag.PATCH /api/admin/cms/<id>. Records a cms.update audit entry. (Key is never sent — it cannot change.)Super Admin Admin Marketing
Delete (edit mode only)Removes the block after a confirmation prompt (“Delete block '…'?”).DELETE /api/admin/cms/<id>. Records a cms.delete audit entry.Super Admin Admin only
Cancel / × / EscCloses the dialog without saving.None.Any who can open it
⛔
Caution — Delete is permanent and Marketing cannot do it

Deleting a block hard-removes the record from the database; there is no soft-delete or undo. Only Admin and Super Admin may delete — a Marketing user who tries gets a 403 (“Only Marketing / Admin can manage CMS”). If a page suddenly loses content, check whether its block was deleted rather than merely unpublished.

✅
Best Practice — draft, preview, then publish

Create or edit a block with Published off, confirm the JSON is right, then flip it on. Because only published blocks reach the storefront, this lets you stage copy safely. Keep keys descriptive and prefixed by area (page.about, banner.hero, legal.privacy) so the storefront's prefix-based hydration finds them.

Typed Block Manager (Pages · Banners · Testimonials · Legal)

A focused pop-up listing only the blocks of one type, opened from the hub tiles.

Purpose Work on one content area at a time — e.g. all the homepage banners together — without scrolling the full blocks table.
Access Same as the blocks manager — Super Admin Admin Marketing (delete Admin/Super only). Path Admin → Content → CMS → (Pages / Banners / Testimonials / Legal tile)
Dialog — Banners · “· N blocks”
Intro line“Content blocks of type banner, stored in the DOODLY database. Published blocks are the ones the storefront reads.”
Block rowsEach row: Key (bold) · Published/Draft badge · a short preview of the JSON · [Edit]
Empty state“No banners blocks yet — create the first one.”
Footer[Close] · [＋ New Banner]
Figure — the typed block manager (Banners shown): a filtered list plus a type-scoped “New” button.

How it works

ElementDetail
What it listsOnly blocks whose type matches the tile — page, banner, testimonial or legal. The count in the title updates to match.
PreviewEach row shows a truncated (~90-character) preview of the block's JSON data, so you can tell blocks apart at a glance.
EditOpens the same Create/Edit dialog from Screen 2, pre-filled with that block.
＋ New <type>Opens the create dialog with the Type already set and the Key pre-seeded with the type prefix (e.g. banner.) so you only add the suffix.
Close / × / click-outsideDismisses the pop-up.
ℹ️
Note — FAQs is different

The FAQs tile does not open a typed block manager. It navigates to the dedicated Help Center admin (/admin/help-center.html), which has its own question/answer/video tooling. Everything else on the hub uses the CmsBlock manager described here.

Product CMS Editor

A tabbed slide-over that edits every attribute of a product and pushes it live across the storefront.

Purpose Launch, price, describe and merchandise a product — 12 tabs covering basics, pricing, variants, subscription plans, nutrition, quality, description, badges, images, availability, SEO and analytics.
Access Super Admin Admin (full products control); reached from the Products screen. Path Admin → Content → CMS → Products & status → Products → Edit
ℹ️
How to open it

Click Products & status on the CMS hub to reach /admin/products.html, then click any product's Edit button. A slide-over panel opens on the right with a tab strip and Save / Cancel / Reset controls. Edits are applied to the live catalogue the whole storefront reads (localStorage overlay in the static build; a PATCH API + cache revalidation in production), so changes reflect with no redeploy.

Slide-over — Product CMS · <product name> · [×]
Tab strip (12 tabs)Basic · Pricing · Variants · Subscriptions · Nutrition · Quality · Description · Badges · Images · Availability · SEO · Analytics
Tab bodyThe fields/table for the selected tab
Footer left[Reset all CMS edits]
Footer right[Cancel] · [Save changes]
Figure — the Product CMS slide-over: a 12-tab editor with a save/reset footer.

Tab 1 · Basic

FieldPurposeAccepted valuesReq.Validation / backendExample
Product nameThe name shown everywhere on the site.TextYesFree text; written to name.A2 Buffalo Milk
SlugThe URL-friendly identifier for the product page.TextOpt.Defaults to the product id if blank; written to slug.a2-buffalo-milk
StatusThe product's lifecycle state — this is the “status flip” that launches a product.Active · Draft · Coming Soon · Out of Stock · DiscontinuedYesDropdown; written to status (values: available / draft / coming_soon / out_of_stock / discontinued).Active
Visible on siteWhether the product is listed in the storefront at all.“Listed in storefront” checkboxOpt.On unless explicitly unchecked; written to visible.On
CategoryGroups the product in the catalogue.TextOpt.Written to category.Milk
Display orderSort position among products (lower shows first).NumberOpt.Written to order; defaults 0.1
From (label)A short “from …” tagline, e.g. a starting price label.TextOpt.Written to from.From ₹90/day
Rating valueThe star rating shown on the card.Number (step 0.1)Opt.Written to rating.value.4.8
Rating countHow many ratings the value is based on.NumberOpt.Written to rating.count.1240

Tab 2 · Pricing

Nine money fields (all numeric, in ₹ except the percentage ones), written under pricing.*.

FieldWrites toMeaning
MRP (₹)pricing.mrpMaximum retail price (strike-through).
Selling (₹)pricing.sellingThe price customers actually pay.
Cost (₹)pricing.costInternal cost price (for margin).
Offer (₹)pricing.offerA special offer price.
Discount %pricing.discountPctDisplayed discount percentage.
Tax %pricing.taxPctGST / tax rate applied.
Glass deposit (₹)pricing.depositRefundable bottle deposit.
Delivery charge (₹)pricing.deliveryChargePer-order delivery fee.
Free delivery over (₹)pricing.freeDeliveryThresholdOrder value above which delivery is free.
⚠️
Warning — pricing is charged live

Because storefront display and checkout both read this catalogue, changing Selling here changes what customers are charged with no further step. Double-check the number before Save.

Tab 3 · Variants

A table of every variant tied to this product. Editable columns: Display name, SKU, Price (labelled /day for normal variants or fixed for a trial variant — it writes to dailyPrice or fixedPrice accordingly), Stock, Weight, and an Active checkbox. If the product has no variants it shows “No variants for this product.”

Tab 4 · Subscriptions (plans)

A table of the shared subscription plans. Columns: Plan (name), Days (duration), Discount (entered as a whole percentage and stored as a fraction), Badge (a promo tag), Auto-renew checkbox and Active checkbox.

Tab 5 · Nutrition

Nine label fields written under nutrition.*: Fat, SNF, Protein, Calcium, Energy, Carbohydrates, Sugar, Minerals, Vitamins.

Tab 6 · Quality

Ten fields written under quality.*: Fat %, SNF, Lactometer, Collection temp, Storage temp, Batch no., Milk type, Animal type, Collection date, Expiry.

Tab 7 · Description

Seven fields written under description.*. The first five are multi-line text areas: Short description, Long description, Product story, Usage instructions, Storage instructions; plus Ingredients and Allergen info.

Tab 8 · Badges

Toggles which trust badges appear on the storefront. Each badge row shows its icon, an editable label, and a Show checkbox. On save, the badge list is rebuilt from the rows and written to the product's badges.

Tab 9 · Images

Manages the product gallery. Each image row shows a thumbnail and filename, a ★ featured radio (picks the main image), and ↑ / ↓ buttons to reorder. On save, the new order is written to gallery and the chosen featured image to image. Changes apply across the whole site.

Tab 10 · Availability

FieldWrites toType
Low-stock threshold (units)lowStockThresholdNumber
Launch date (YYYY-MM-DD)launchDateText (date)
Restock date (YYYY-MM-DD)restockDateText (date)
Available citiesavailability.citiesComma-separated list
Delivery slotsavailability.slotsComma-separated list
End dateavailability.endDateText
Inventory statusavailability.inventoryStatusText

List fields accept comma-separated values (e.g. “Hyderabad, Bengaluru”) and are stored as arrays.

Tab 11 · SEO

Five fields under seo.*: Meta title, Meta description (text area), OG image, Canonical URL, and Keywords (comma-separated list).

Tab 12 · Analytics

Read-only. Six KPI cards — Total orders, Revenue, Views, Conversion, Stock remaining, Rating — with a note that they are “populated from orders/payments in production”.

Footer buttons

ButtonWhat it doesBackend processPermission
Save changesWrites every edited field across all tabs (products, variants, plans, badges, gallery, featured image) to the live catalogue, refreshes the products table, and shows a “Saved — live across the storefront” toast.Applies the field overrides through the CMS layer (localStorage overlay here; PATCH + cache revalidation in production).Super Admin Admin
Cancel / × / scrim clickCloses the slide-over without saving.None.Same
Reset all CMS editsClears every stored CMS override and reloads the page, restoring the original catalogue values.Wipes the CMS override store, then reloads.Super Admin Admin
⛔
Caution — “Reset all CMS edits” is global

Reset does not undo only this product — it discards every product/variant/plan CMS edit and reloads. Use it only when you genuinely want to return the whole catalogue to its baseline. There is no per-product undo.

Workflow — a content edit reaching the storefront
Open CMSPick a tile / block / productEdit fields (or JSON)Set Published / StatusSave (writes CmsBlock or catalogue)Audit entry recordedStorefront reads published content on next load
Figure — from an admin edit to live customer-facing content.
ℹ️
Note — how blocks reach a page

Storefront prose sections (About, Farmers, Bottle-Returns, etc.) carry data-cms="<key>" wrappers. After the page renders, the site fetches the published blocks for each key-prefix on that page via /api/cms/page?prefix=<prefix> and overlays their eyebrow, heading, text and html fields onto the DOM. If the backend is unreachable it silently keeps the built-in copy — so a page never goes blank. This is why block keys are prefixed by area (e.g. about.hero, about.story).

Troubleshooting

SymptomLikely causeFix
Red banner “⚠ Your role can't view CMS (403)”.You are signed in as a role without cms:view (only Super Admin, Admin, Marketing have it).Ask a Super Admin to grant CMS access, or sign in with an authorised account.
Red banner “⚠ Backend offline — start next-app”.The Next.js backend that serves /api/admin/cms is not running or unreachable.Start / restore the backend, then reload the page.
Save fails with “A block with that key already exists.”Another block already uses that Key (keys are unique).Choose a different Key, or edit the existing block instead of creating a new one.
Save fails with “Data must be valid JSON.”The Data box contains malformed JSON (missing quote/brace/comma).Fix the JSON — every key and string value must be double-quoted; no trailing commas.
Delete fails with “Only Marketing / Admin (403).”Delete needs Admin/Super Admin; a Marketing user cannot delete blocks.Ask an Admin to delete it, or just un-publish the block to hide it instead.
Edited a block but the storefront still shows old copy.The block is saved as a Draft (Published off), or the page/section key-prefix does not match.Turn Published on; confirm the block Key's prefix matches the page's data-cms wrapper.
A product edit didn't stick / everything reverted.Someone clicked “Reset all CMS edits”, which clears every override globally.Re-enter the product edits; reserve Reset for a genuine full rollback.

Frequently asked questions

Do I need to redeploy the site after a content change?

No. Published content blocks are read at page load, and product edits apply to the live catalogue immediately. That is the whole point of the CMS — copy, banners, prices and product status change with no deploy.

What is the difference between “un-publish” and “delete”?

Un-publishing (Published off) keeps the block in the database but hides it from customers — fully reversible. Deleting removes the record permanently and is Admin/Super-Admin-only. Prefer un-publish unless you are sure the block is gone for good.

Why can I create and edit blocks but not delete them?

Your role is probably Marketing. Marketing has “manage” rights on CMS (view, create, edit, export) but not delete. Delete is reserved for Admin and Super Admin.

Can I change a block's Key later?

No. The Key is locked once a block exists (the field is disabled in edit mode) because the storefront looks blocks up by Key. If you need a different key, create a new block and delete the old one.

Where do I actually launch a new product?

On the Products & status tile → Products screen → the product's Edit → Product CMS editor → Basic tab → set Status to Active and Visible on site on, then Save.

✅
Best Practice — every change is audited

Creating, editing and deleting content blocks each write an audit entry (cms.create / cms.update / cms.delete) tied to the acting role. If live copy changes unexpectedly, the Audit Logs screen tells you who changed which block and when — use it before assuming a bug.

DOODLYAdmin · Content · Brand Story
§Admin · Content

Brand Story

Edit the headline copy and packaging-QR of the public “UNFOLD PURE.” story page (/doodly.html) — and download the QR that goes on your milk-bottle labels — without touching any code. Changes go live instantly.

ℹ️
Where this lives

Open it from Admin → Content → Brand Story (URL /admin/brand-story.html). It is a small content-management screen that edits one thing: the storefront brand-story page at /doodly.html (titled “UNFOLD PURE.” — the digital version of the insert printed inside DOODLY packaging). The editor is loaded from, and saved back to, the DOODLY database through the endpoint /api/brand-story. When it loads cleanly you see a green banner: “● Live — brand story loaded from the DOODLY database … Edits persist to Postgres.” A red banner means the backend is offline or your role is not allowed to edit — see Troubleshooting.

ℹ️
What this page is NOT

This is not a full drag-and-drop page builder. The long story panels (Market Reality, Why DOODLY, the farm journey, Trust, contact channels) are fixed in the product and are not edited here. This screen lets you override a small set of headline fields and the QR destination. Everything you leave blank keeps its built-in default.

Brand Story Editor

A single screen split into two panels — an Editable content form on the left and a Packaging QR panel on the right.

Purpose Change the hero headline copy and the products heading on the public story page, set where the printed QR code points, and download that QR as an image for your packaging — all live, with no deployment.
Access Super Admin Admin Marketing can load and edit. Any other role is blocked at the backend (403) and can only tinker locally — nothing they change reaches the live page. Path Admin → Content → Brand Story
Header — Brand Story — Unfold Pure · “Edit the /doodly story hero & CTAs and download the packaging QR.”
Live status bannerGreen “● Live — brand story loaded from the DOODLY database …” on success; red warning if offline or blocked
Left panel — Editable contentSix text fields (Hero eyebrow · Hero title · Hero lead · Hero sub-line · Products heading · QR destination URL), each showing its default as a grey placeholder
Left panel — actions[Save changes] · [Reset to defaults] · transient “✓ Saved” tick
Right panel — Packaging QRLive QR preview (190×190) · the destination URL in text · [Download SVG] · [Download PNG] · “Preview the live page →” link
Figure — the Brand Story editor: content form on the left, packaging-QR tools on the right.

How editing works

Each field is an override. Type a value to replace the built-in text on the live page; leave a field blank to keep the built-in default, which is shown to you as the field's grey placeholder. There is nothing to “publish” separately — Save changes writes straight to the live page and the DOODLY database. The screen tells you exactly this above the form: “Leave a field blank to use the built-in default (shown as placeholder). Saved instantly to the live page — no code change.”

The six editable fields (left panel)

Every field below is real and appears on this screen. The “Where it shows” column tells you exactly what part of the public /doodly.html page it controls.

FieldPurpose / where it showsAccepted valuesReq.Validation / backendExample
Hero eyebrowThe small line of text with a ✦ above the giant hero title, at the very top of the story.Short free text.Opt.Trimmed. Blank keeps the default. Stored server-side as heroEyebrow.The DOODLY packaging insert, reborn digitally
Hero titleThe huge headline at the top of the page (the brand promise).Short free text — a few words.Opt.Trimmed. Placeholder is the default “UNFOLD PURE.”. Stored as heroTitle.UNFOLD PURE.
Hero leadThe first line directly under the hero title.Short sentence.Opt.Trimmed. Blank keeps “This is not just dairy.” Stored as heroLead.This is not just dairy.
Hero sub-lineThe second, smaller line under the hero title.Short sentence.Opt.Trimmed. Blank keeps “A story of honesty, freshness, and trust.” Stored as heroSub.A story of honesty, freshness, and trust.
Products headingThe heading over the “Our Products” section further down the story.Short free text.Opt.Trimmed; updates the page locally. See the caution below — this one field is not persisted to the central database.Our Premium Collection
QR destination URLThe web address the printed packaging QR opens when scanned. Also drives the live QR preview in the right panel.A full URL.Opt.Trimmed. Blank defaults to your site's /doodly.html. Stored as qrDestination. The QR preview redraws as you type.https://doodly.in/doodly.html
⚠️
“Products heading” saves locally, but not to the database

The backend deliberately accepts only a fixed whitelist of fields (heroEyebrow, heroTitle, heroLead, heroSub, hookTitle, hookLine, qrDestination, and the three CTA links). Products heading is not on that list. When you save it, it updates the page in your own browser immediately, but the DOODLY database drops it — so after a hard reload (which reloads the copy from the database) or on any other device, the Products heading reverts to its default “Our Premium Collection”. To change it permanently, ask a developer to update the source. Treat this field as a preview, not a permanent setting.

Buttons on this screen

ButtonWhat it doesBackend processPermission
Save changesSaves every non-blank field. Shows a green “✓ Saved” tick for ~2 seconds and pops a toast.Collects the filled fields into a patch, mirrors it into the browser, then sends PUT /api/brand-story. On success the toast reads “Saved to the DOODLY database”. The page at /doodly.html picks up the change on its next load.Marketing Admin Super Admin
Reset to defaultsClears every override so the page falls back to its built-in copy. The form empties and the placeholders return.Wipes the local override and sends an empty patch via PUT /api/brand-story. Toast reads “Reset — saved to the DOODLY database”.Marketing Admin Super Admin
Download SVGDownloads the packaging QR as a crisp vector file, doodly-qr.svg — best for print and large signage.Client-side only. Renders the current QR destination to an SVG and triggers the download. No save.Marketing+
Download PNGDownloads the QR as a high-resolution image, doodly-qr.png — best for slides, social and quick sharing.Client-side only. Renders the QR at 16× scale to a PNG and downloads it. No save.Marketing+
Preview the live page →Opens the actual public story page, /doodly.html, in a new browser tab so you can check your edit.None — it is a plain link.Marketing+ (the page itself is public)
✓ Saved indicatorNot a button — a small confirmation tick that appears after a successful Save and fades after about 2 seconds.None.—
💡
Tip — the QR preview is live

As you type in the QR destination URL box, the QR image in the right panel redraws in real time and the URL under it updates. So you can point the code at a campaign landing page, watch the preview change, then hit Download PNG/SVG to hand the exact code to your packaging printer — no third-party QR site needed.

The Packaging QR panel (right side)

ElementDetail
QR previewA 190×190 live render of the code for the current destination, drawn in the DOODLY dark-green ink. It updates instantly as you edit the destination field.
Destination textThe full URL the code points to, printed below the preview so you can read and verify it at a glance.
Download SVG / Download PNGTwo download buttons (see the button table above) that save the code as a print-ready vector or a high-res image.
Preview the live page →A full-width link that opens /doodly.html in a new tab.
Workflow — how an edit reaches the live page
Open Brand Story Backend loads saved overrides (GET /api/brand-story) You edit a field Save changes Patch saved to DOODLY database (PUT) /doodly.html reads it on next load
Figure — an edit is saved as an override patch, stored in the database, and applied over the built-in copy the next time the story page loads.

How your changes are stored and applied

Behind the scenes, your edits are not a full copy of the page — they are a small override stored as a single record in the DOODLY database (the “brand story config”, a singleton row). When the public page renders, it starts from the built-in defaults and merges your overrides on top. That is why a blank field simply keeps the default: there is no override to apply. It also means you can never “break” the page by clearing a field — the worst case is that it shows the original text.

ℹ️
Only safe text is stored

When you save, the backend sanitises the payload: it keeps only the recognised fields, and only non-empty trimmed strings. Anything unexpected is silently dropped. This keeps the story page safe no matter what is typed in.

ℹ️
A short cache delay is normal

Because the public story page is cached for speed, a saved change can take a few minutes to appear for everyone. If your own Preview the live page still shows the old text, give it a moment and refresh — the parallel implementation states edits go live “within 10 minutes”.

The page this screen controls — /doodly.html (“UNFOLD PURE.”)

Editing here changes the public brand-story page. It is a long, single-scroll “story” with a side navigation, and it is completely public (customers reach it from packaging QR scans and links). It is built from these parts, top to bottom:

Part of the pageWhat it isEditable here?
HeroThe full-screen opener: the ✦ eyebrow, the giant title, the lead and sub-line, and “Begin the story ↓ / Order Now” buttons.Yes — eyebrow, title, lead, sub-line.
Farm sceneAn animated “From our farms to your morning” illustration (buffalo, tractor, dairy crate, sunrise) with the DOODLY logo and floating fact cards.No.
Market RealitySection 1 — the problem with most dairy (stored too long, treated, far from source).No.
Why DOODLYSection 2 — A2 buffalo milk, farm-fresh in 12 hours, and the 5-step farm-to-door journey.No.
Our ProductsSection 3 — a grid of products (milk, curd, paneer, ghee, palkova) that automatically show “Coming soon” from the live catalogue.Heading only (locally — see the caution above).
TrustSection 4 — the “for kids / parents / grandparents” trust lines.No.
Scan & ConnectSection 5 — the on-page QR code (pointing at your QR destination URL), the contact channels (website, WhatsApp, phone, email, Instagram, hours) and the call-to-action buttons.QR destination, yes. Channels/CTAs, no (in this editor).
ℹ️
The QR you download and the QR on the page match

The QR destination URL you set here drives both the code shown in the “Scan & Connect” section of the live page and the SVG/PNG you download for packaging. Set it once and everything stays in sync.

✅
Best Practice

Keep the QR destination URL pointed at your own /doodly.html story page (the default) unless you are running a specific campaign — that way every bottle scanned lands on the trust-building story, and you can change where it goes later without reprinting. Edit hero copy sparingly and in short, punchy lines (this is a hero, not a paragraph). After any change, click Preview the live page and read it on both desktop and phone before you consider it done. Before a packaging print run, always re-download a fresh QR here so the printed code matches the current destination.

Who can do what

Brand Story is governed by the CMS (content) permission. Unusually for an admin screen, viewing and editing need the same permission — there is no view-only tier here, because loading the editor already reads the live overrides.

RoleLoad the editorSave / reset (persist to database)
Super AdminYesYes
AdminYesYes
MarketingYesYes
Accountant · Manager · other staff rolesNo — the backend returns 403 and shows a red bannerNo
Delivery Executive · CustomerNo — they never reach /adminNo
⚠️
A blocked role can still type — but nothing saves
