Each line carries a role — user (customer), bot (Assistant), agent (a staff reply), note (an internal note) or system (automatic events) — plus the author name, the body text, an optional detected intent, and the time it was created.

Every server action

ActionWhat it doesSurfaced in this UI?Permission
GET view=listLists conversations (optionally with messages); supports query, status, handler, assignee, date-range, sort and paging filters.Yes — this is what fills the Conversations table (up to 200 with messages).view
GET view=dashboardReturns the KPI totals and top topics.Powers the Dashboard figures.view
GET view=detailReturns one chat with its full message timeline.Behind the transcript view.view
sendPosts an agent reply into the chat (flips it to Human).API — not a button on this screen yet.manage
noteAdds an internal note (not shown to the customer).API only.manage
assignAssigns the chat to a staff member.Yes — Assign to me.manage
transferReassigns to another agent and reactivates the chat.API only.manage
statusSets any status directly (Active/Waiting/Resolved/Escalated/Closed).API only.manage
closeResolves the chat.Yes — Mark resolved.manage
reopenRe-activates a resolved chat.API only.manage
escalateFlags the chat as escalated to a human.API only (customers also trigger this from the widget).manage
csatRecords a 1–5 satisfaction rating.API only.manage
delete / restoreSoft-deletes or restores a chat.API only.manage
bulkApplies delete/restore or a status to many chats at once.API only.manage
logRecords an export or other event in the audit trail.API only.view
ℹ️
Everything is audited

Every mutation writes an entry to the shared Audit Log as chat.<action> (for example chat.close, chat.assign). Status changes and assignments also drop a System message into the transcript itself, so the conversation carries its own history of who did what and when.

✅
Best Practice — assign before you reply, resolve when you're done

When you step into a customer chat, click Assign to me first. That flips the chat to Human-handled and stamps your name on it, so a teammate can see at a glance that it's covered and won't double-answer. When the issue is settled, click Mark resolved — it closes the chat, clears any escalation flag, and moves it out of the Active count. Both actions leave a System note in the transcript, giving you a clean, auditable record of ownership and outcome.

✅
Best Practice — fix a wrong answer in seconds with a Knowledge Base override

If the assistant is giving an outdated or off-brand answer (say, an old price or delivery cut-off), don't wait for a code change. Open the Knowledge Base tab, copy the topic id from the table, and paste a corrected Override answer. It goes live to the storefront widget the moment you save. Once the underlying answer is properly updated, Remove the override so you fall back to the tested default.

Troubleshooting

SymptomLikely causeFix
The page shows "Access restricted — You don't have permission to view chat support."Your role lacks the chatSupport view permission.Sign in as Super Admin or Admin, or have the Super Admin grant your role chatSupport:view (Support and roles with customers:view can also view via the server).
A red banner reads "Backend offline… start next-app".The API server isn't reachable.Start the next-app backend. Until it responds, the page shows the last synced copy of the chats.
Clicking Mark resolved / Assign to me shows "Only Support / Admin can manage chats (403)."Your role can view chats but not manage them.Managing needs chatSupport:edit or support:edit. Ask the Super Admin to grant it, or have a Support/Admin user act.
The Conversations table is empty on a live site.No customers have chatted yet; sample chats are never seeded in production.Expected. The list fills as real conversations happen. Confirm the backend banner is green so you know it's connected.
The Dashboard counts look stale.Auto-refresh pauses while a transcript or pop-up is open, and only runs on the Dashboard tab.Close any open chat, return to the Dashboard tab, and it refreshes within 20 seconds — or reopen the page to force a fresh fetch.
"Enter a topic id and answer" when saving an override.One of the two Knowledge Base fields was blank.Fill both the Topic id (exactly as shown in the topics table) and the Override answer, then Save.
A chat shows Active even though it looks finished.A database "Waiting" chat is displayed as Active in this view.Open it and Mark resolved when the issue is genuinely closed; the badge then turns green.

Frequently asked questions

Where do these conversations come from?

From the Doodly Assistant — the chat button on every storefront and customer page. Each conversation is saved to the DOODLY database and mirrored into this admin view. You are reading real customer chats, not a simulation.

Can I type a reply to the customer from this screen?

The current admin screen focuses on reading a chat and two actions — Mark resolved and Assign to me. The backend already supports agent replies and internal notes (the send and note actions), which flip a chat to Human-handled; those are available to tooling built on the API. For live back-and-forth, the assistant hands customers to WhatsApp, phone or a callback.

What's the difference between "Escalated" and "Assigned"?

Escalated means the chat was flagged for a human (often because the customer asked to talk to someone). Assigned means a specific staff member owns it. You typically assign an escalated chat to yourself, which also switches Handled by to Human.

If I delete a chat, is it gone forever?

No. Deletes are soft — the record is marked deleted (hidden from the list) but preserved and can be restored. This keeps your history and audit trail intact.

Does changing a Knowledge Base override affect what customers see immediately?

Yes. Once you save an override, the assistant uses your wording for that topic straight away on the live widget. Remove the override to return to the built-in answer.

Why does "Avg response time" always say 1.0s?

The assistant answers instantly from its knowledge base, so the dashboard shows a fixed, representative 1.0s as a headline. It is an indicative figure for how fast the AI replies, not a per-conversation stopwatch.

DOODLYSystem · User Management
§System

User Management

Create staff accounts, assign each person a role, lock or disable access, reset passwords, and soft-delete people who leave — every change is saved to the DOODLY database and written to the audit log.

The User Management page is where you decide who can sign in to DOODLY and what they are. You do not set individual permissions here — those come from the person's role (managed on Admin → System → Roles & Permissions). This page is about the accounts themselves: adding a new team member, changing someone's role, temporarily locking an account, disabling a leaver, forcing a password reset, and removing an account safely. Deletes are always soft — the record is hidden and the account is disabled, but nothing is truly erased, so mistakes can be undone.

The screen is made of two stacked parts that load together: a statistics strip across the top (live counts of your team), and the user directory table below it (one row per account, with all the action buttons). We document each part, then the Create / Edit user form that both the "Create user" button and every row's pencil button open.

ℹ️
Where the data lives

When the DOODLY backend is running, this page reads and writes real accounts through /api/users (Postgres). You will see a green "● Live — N user(s) from the DOODLY database" banner. If the backend is offline you get an amber warning and the page falls back to a local sample list — changes then only affect your browser, not the real system.

User statistics strip

Five live counters summarising your whole team at a glance.

Purpose Give a one-look health check of the account base — how many people you have, how many can actually log in, and how much sign-in activity happened today — before you drill into the table.
Access Super Admin and any role granted the User Management → View permission. If your role cannot view Users, the whole strip stays hidden. Path Admin → System → User Management (top of page)
Header — User Management · "Create staff, assign roles, lock or disable accounts. Deletes are soft."
Total UsersCount of all non-deleted accounts
ActiveAccounts with status = active
Disabled / LockedAccounts that cannot sign in right now
RolesHow many distinct roles are in use · click → Roles & Permissions
Logins TodaySign-ins recorded today · click → Audit Logs
Figure — the five KPI tiles that sit above the user table
TileWhat it countsClickable?Where it comes from
Total UsersEvery account that has not been soft-deleted.NoNon-deleted rows from the user list.
ActiveAccounts whose status is active (can sign in normally).Nostatus = active.
Disabled / LockedAccounts that are either disabled or locked — i.e. currently blocked from signing in.Nostatus = disabled or locked.
RolesNumber of different roles currently assigned across the team.Yes → Admin → System → Roles & PermissionsDistinct roles among non-deleted users.
Logins TodayHow many successful sign-ins were recorded so far today.Yes → Admin → System → Audit LogsToday's auth.login audit events.
💡
Tip

The strip refreshes automatically after every action you take in the table below (create, lock, disable, reset, delete, restore). You never need to reload the page to see the counters update.

User directory

The full list of accounts, with per-row buttons to edit, lock, disable, reset and delete.

Purpose The working list of every staff and customer account. From here you open the create form, and act on any single account without leaving the page.
Access Super Admin — view needs Users → View; the row buttons need Edit / Delete. Super Admin accounts can only be managed by another Super Admin. Path Admin → System → User Management
Toolbar — + Create user · "N users · roles assignable · soft-delete"
UserFull name (bold) + email underneath
RoleColour-coded role badge
StatusGreen = active · Red = locked · Amber = disabled
Last loginDate of the person's last sign-in (or —)
ActionsEdit · Lock/Unlock · Disable/Enable · Reset pw · Delete
Figure — one row per account; the toolbar's Create user button opens the form covered below

Table specification

AspectBehaviour in this table
ColumnsUser (name + email), Role, Status, Last login, Actions.
Rows shownAll accounts that are not soft-deleted. Deleted accounts drop out of the list once removed.
SearchServer-side search is available on the backend (?q= matches name, email or phone); the on-page table itself shows the loaded set (up to 500 accounts) without a separate search box.
FilterThe backend supports a ?role= filter and an includeDeleted=1 flag; the page loads with deleted accounts included so it can offer Restore.
SortingNewest accounts first (by creation date) as returned by the database.
PaginationLoads up to 500 users in one go (the standard team size fits easily).
Row actionsEdit, Lock / Unlock, Disable / Enable, Reset pw, Delete (soft) / Restore.
ExportsNone on this screen — user data is sensitive. Use Admin → System → Audit Logs for a change history.

Status badges

BadgeColourMeaningCan the person sign in?
activeGreenNormal, working account.Yes
lockedRedTemporarily blocked — usually after suspicious activity or on request. Meant to be reversed.No
disabledAmberSwitched off — e.g. a leaver, or an account not yet in use. Also set automatically when an account is deleted.No

Row action buttons

ButtonWhat it doesBackend processPermission
✎ EditOpens the user form pre-filled so you can change the name, role or status.PATCH /api/users/{id} with the changed fields.Super Admin+ (Users → Edit)
Lock / UnlockToggles the account between active and locked. Use to freeze access fast without deleting anything.PATCH /api/users/{id} → status: LOCKED or ACTIVE.Super Admin+ (Users → Edit)
Disable / EnableToggles the account between active and disabled. Use for leavers or accounts to switch off longer-term.PATCH /api/users/{id} → status: DISABLED or ACTIVE.Super Admin+ (Users → Edit)
Reset pwForces a password reset — the person must set a new password at their next sign-in. No password is shown to you.POST /api/users/{id}/reset-password with force:true → flips forcePwReset on. Logged as auth.password_reset.Super Admin+ (Users → Edit)
🗑 DeleteSoft-deletes the account after a confirm prompt. The row disappears from the list; the account is disabled but not erased.DELETE /api/users/{id} → sets status: DISABLED and stamps deletedAt.Super Admin+ (Users → Delete)
RestoreFor an already-deleted account, brings it back to active. (The Delete button becomes Restore for soft-deleted accounts.)PATCH /api/users/{id} → restore:true (clears deletedAt, sets status active). Logged as user.restore.Super Admin+ (Users → Edit)
⚠️
Super Admin accounts are protected

Only a Super Admin can edit, change the role of, reset the password for, or delete a Super Admin account. If you are an Admin (not Super Admin) and try, the backend returns "Only a Super Admin can manage Super Admin accounts." (403). This stops a lower-privileged admin from tampering with the top account.

ℹ️
Lock vs Disable vs Delete

Lock = quick, reversible freeze (e.g. security concern). Disable = switch off a leaver or dormant account. Delete = remove from the list (still recoverable via Restore, since deletes are soft). Only the person's role — not these status buttons — controls what they can do once signed in.

Create / Edit user form

The pop-up that adds a new account or changes an existing one.

Purpose Add a new team member with a role, or update an existing person's name, role and status. On create, DOODLY generates a secure temporary password and requires the person to change it at first login.
Access Super Admin+ — creating needs Users → Create; editing needs Users → Edit. Only a Super Admin may create or set the Super Admin role. Path Admin → System → User Management → Create user (or the ✎ Edit button on a row)
Modal — Create user / Edit user · ✕ close
Full nameFree-text field
EmailLogin identity (unique)
RoleDropdown of all roles
Statusactive · disabled · locked
Force password resetCheckbox — create only, ticked by default
Create user / Save changesPrimary button that saves to the database
Figure — the create form; when editing, the same form opens pre-filled and the email is fixed

Field reference

FieldPurposeAccepted valuesReq.Validation / backendExample
Full nameThe person's display name across DOODLY.Text, 1–80 characters.YesCannot be blank (the form focuses this field if empty). Saved to User.name.Priya Nair
EmailThe account's unique login identity; also where a reset link would go.A valid email address.YesMust be a valid, unique email. Lower-cased on the server; duplicate email is rejected with a conflict message. Cannot be changed after creation (read-only when editing).priya.support@doodly.in
PhoneOptional contact / secondary unique identity (backend-supported).7–15 digits, may start with +.Opt.Validated by pattern; duplicate phone is rejected. Stored on User.phone.+91 98765 43210
RoleSets everything the person can see and do — this is the security decision.One of the 11 DOODLY roles (see the role list below).YesMust be a known role. Choosing Super Admin is allowed only if you are a Super Admin. Stored on User.role.Customer Support
StatusWhether the account can sign in from the moment it is created / saved.active, disabled or locked.YesMaps to ACTIVE / DISABLED / LOCKED on the server.active
Force password reset on first loginMakes the new person choose their own password before they can use the account.Checkbox — ticked by default, create only.Opt.New accounts always get forcePwReset = true on the server, so the person must set a new password at first sign-in regardless.Ticked
ℹ️
You never type the new user's password

On Create, DOODLY generates a strong random temporary password automatically and shows it to you once in a pop-up (for example DyKp7mNq2rX7). Copy it and share it with the new person securely — they will be forced to replace it at first login. The password never appears again.

⚠️
Password policy (enforced by the server)

Any password DOODLY sets or a user chooses must be at least 8 characters (max 72) and include an uppercase letter, a lowercase letter, a number, and a special character. The auto-generated temporary password already meets this.

Form buttons

ButtonWhat it doesBackend processPermission
Create userValidates the form, creates the account, and shows the one-time temporary password.POST /api/users with name, email, role and a generated password; sets forcePwReset. Returns the new account (HTTP 201). Logged as user.create.Super Admin+ (Users → Create)
Save changes (edit mode)Saves the edited name, role and status. Email stays as-is.PATCH /api/users/{id} with the changed fields. Logged as user.update.Super Admin+ (Users → Edit)
✕ CloseDismisses the form without saving. Clicking the dark backdrop also closes it.No backend call.—
Workflow — onboarding a new team member
Click Create userEnter name + emailPick role + statusCreate userCopy temp passwordShare securelyUser sets own password at first login
Figure — end-to-end flow from creating the account to the new person signing in
Workflow — offboarding a leaver
Find the person in the tableDisable (or Lock) to block access nowDelete when confirmed → soft-deleteAccount hidden + disabledRestore later if it was a mistake
Figure — safe removal that can always be reversed

The 11 DOODLY roles you can assign

The Role dropdown offers these roles. The role is what actually grants access — fine-grained permissions per role live on the Roles & Permissions and Permissions pages.

Role (as shown)Typical useSigns in to
Super AdminFull, unrestricted access to everything; can manage other Super Admins. Assignable only by a Super Admin.Admin
AdminBroad administrative access across the business (short of the Super-Admin-only guards).Admin
Customer SupportHandles tickets, chats and customer queries.Admin (lands on Support Tickets)
Operations ManagerRuns deliveries and day-to-day operations.Admin (lands on Delivery Mgmt)
Procurement ManagerManages farmers and milk procurement.Admin (lands on Procurement)
AccountantFinance, revenue and billing.Admin (lands on Revenue)
Inventory ManagerStock and bottle inventory.Admin (lands on Inventory)
Quality ControlMilk quality testing and approvals.Admin (lands on Quality)
Marketing ManagerCoupons, offers and growth content.Admin (lands on Coupons)
Delivery ExecutiveField delivery staff using the driver app.Driver app
CustomerAn end customer of DOODLY (not staff).Customer account area
✅
Best Practice

Give every person the lowest role that lets them do their job (least privilege). Reserve Admin and Super Admin for the few who genuinely need them. When someone leaves, Disable or Lock immediately to cut access, then Delete once you have confirmed nothing else depends on the account — because deletes are soft, you can always Restore if you acted too soon. Share temporary passwords over a secure channel, never in a public chat or email thread.

Troubleshooting

SymptomLikely causeFix
Amber banner: "Backend offline — start next-app" and changes don't stick.The DOODLY backend isn't running, so the page is using a local sample list.Start the backend (next-app). Once the green "● Live" banner appears, your create/edit/lock/delete actions save to the real database.
"An account with this email already exists."The email (or phone) is already used by another account, including a soft-deleted one.Search for the existing account and edit or restore it instead of creating a duplicate. Use a different email if it is genuinely a new person.
"Only a Super Admin can manage Super Admin accounts." (403)You are an Admin trying to edit, delete, reset or re-role a Super Admin account.Ask a Super Admin to make the change. This guard is intentional.
"Not allowed for your role (403)."Your role lacks the Users create/edit/delete permission.Have a Super Admin grant your role the relevant User Management permission on the Roles & Permissions page.
New user can't sign in with the password I gave them.New accounts are forced to reset the password at first login.Have them use the temporary password to start, then set their own when prompted. If they lost it, use Reset pw on their row.
I deleted the wrong person.Delete is a soft-delete, not a permanent one.The account still exists as disabled with a deletion timestamp — use Restore to bring it back active.

Frequently asked questions

Does deleting a user permanently erase their data?

No. Delete is a soft-delete: the account is set to disabled and stamped with a deletion time, then hidden from the list. Nothing is truly removed, and you can bring it back with Restore. This protects order history and audit trails tied to the account.

What is the difference between locking and disabling an account?

Both stop the person signing in. Lock is meant as a quick, reversible freeze (for example after a security scare) and toggles straight back to active with one click. Disable is for switching an account off more deliberately, such as for a leaver. Choose whichever matches your intent — neither erases anything.

Can I set a user's password myself?

Not from this screen. On Create, DOODLY generates a strong temporary password and shows it once. To change an existing user's password, use Reset pw, which forces them to choose a new password at their next sign-in. You never see or store their real password — that keeps DOODLY secure.

Why can't I change someone's email after creating them?

The email is the account's login identity and must stay unique and stable. When you edit a user, the email field is fixed — you can change the name, role and status, but not the email. If an email is genuinely wrong, create a fresh account and delete the old one.

Who can assign the Super Admin role?

Only an existing Super Admin. An Admin can create and manage most accounts, but any action that grants, changes, resets or removes a Super Admin is blocked with a 403 for anyone below Super Admin.

Where do I actually change what a role can do?

Not here — this page only assigns a role to a person. To change the permissions attached to a role, go to Admin → System → Roles & Permissions (and the Permissions matrix). Every account with that role then inherits the updated access.

⛔
Caution

Never remove or delete the last active Super Admin account — that role is the only one that can manage other Super Admins and unlock the highest-level settings. Always confirm at least one working Super Admin remains before locking, disabling or deleting one.

DOODLYAdmin · System · Roles & Permissions
§Admin · System

Roles & Permissions

The single control room where the Super Admin decides who can see and do what across the whole platform — set the defaults for a whole job role, fine-tune one single action, or grant an exception to one individual person, and see every change logged.

ℹ️
What this module does, in plain words

Every employee in DOODLY belongs to a role (Admin, Accountant, Operations Manager, Delivery Executive, and so on). A role is simply a saved bundle of permissions. This screen lets you (a) manage the list of roles, (b) tune exactly which actions each role is allowed on each part of the system, and (c) give one specific person a little extra — or a little less — than their role normally gets. The golden formula the platform follows is: Effective permission = the role's default  ⊕  any explicit grant on the role  ⊕  that person's individual override. The most specific setting always wins, and the person's own override beats everything.

⛔
Caution — this is the master key to the building

This is one of the most powerful screens in DOODLY. A wrong setting here can lock a whole team out of the tools they need, or hand someone access to money, customer data or the audit trail that they should never see. Because of that, only the Super Admin can open it. Every other role that navigates here sees a locked message: "Roles & Permissions is restricted — only the Super Admin can manage roles and user-level permission overrides." Read the screen carefully, change one thing at a time, and use the Audit tab and Run tests button to confirm the result.

✅
The three layers of access — remember this order

1. Role level (broad). A "level" for each module: None → View → Manage → Full. This is the everyday tool, edited mostly on the sibling Permissions page. 2. Granular role grant (medium). On the Role Matrix tab you can switch one exact action (say "Delete" on Reports) on or off for a whole role, overriding the level. These show in amber. 3. User override (narrow). On the User Permissions tab you grant or revoke one action for one person only. A user override always wins over the role default.

Roles & Permissions — the four tabs

One workspace split into Roles, Role Matrix, User Permissions and Audit, with a built-in test button.

Purpose Give the Super Admin one place to create and shape roles, tune permissions down to a single action, grant person-specific exceptions, and review the history of every change.
Access Super Admin only — everyone else sees a read-only locked notice Path Admin → System → Roles & Permissions (/admin/roles.html)
Header — Roles & Permissions · tab bar (Roles · Role Matrix · User Permissions · Audit) · Run tests button + result badge (far right)
Tab: RolesThe list of every role — create, duplicate, edit the matrix, assign users, delete custom roles.
Tab: Role MatrixPer-action grid for one chosen role — toggle View / Create / Edit / Delete / Export / Approve / Print and the "special" actions on each module.
Tab: User PermissionsPick one person and grant / revoke individual actions that beat their role default.
Tab: AuditThe history of every role and permission change — who, what, old → new, when.
Run testsA safe self-check that runs 12 built-in access-control tests and reports "N/N tests passed".
Live database bannerA green "● Live — N role(s) in the DOODLY database" strip confirming create / clone / delete are saving to Postgres.
Figure — labelled layout of the Roles & Permissions workspace
💡
Tip — how this page relates to its two neighbours

Three System screens work together. User Management (/admin/users.html) is where you create staff accounts and pick their role. Permissions (/admin/permissions.html) is a simpler grid for setting each role's broad level (None/View/Manage/Full) per module. This screen (Roles & Permissions) is the advanced control — it manages the roles themselves and lets you go finer than a level, down to a single action or a single person. The green banner on this page even reminds you: edit the broad module levels on the Permissions page.

ℹ️
The permission "vocabulary" — 16 action types

Under the hood, DOODLY recognises 16 distinct actions: View, Create, Edit, Delete, Export, Import, Approve, Assign, Reassign, Cancel, Restore, Print, Download, Manage Settings, Manage Users, Manage Roles. Not every action applies to every module — the grid only shows the ones that make sense (for example Assign and Reassign appear on Delivery Mgmt, Drivers and Routes; Cancel and Restore on Orders and Subscriptions; Manage Roles only on this screen and Permissions). Seven of them — View, Create, Edit, Delete, Export, Approve, Print — are the "core" columns; anything extra shows under the Special column.

The eleven built-in roles

Every DOODLY account belongs to exactly one role. Here is what each one is for, out of the box.

Purpose Understand the ready-made roles before you edit anything — most day-to-day needs are already covered by a default role.
Where defined The code default matrix in assets/js/rbac.js (client) and next-app/lib/rbac.ts (server — the real security boundary)
RoleSurface (which app they land in)Landing page after loginWhat they can do by default
Super Admin Full accessAdmin/admin/dashboard.htmlEverything, everywhere — the matrix stores * for this role, so it can never be edited or reduced.
AdminAdmin/admin/dashboard.htmlFull access to almost every module, but the four "critical reserved" ones are held back: Permissions (none), Settings (view), Roles (view), Audit Logs (view).
Customer Support (key: support)Admin/admin/support.htmlFull on Support Tickets; manage Orders; view Dashboard, Customers and Subscriptions.
Operations Manager (operations)Admin/admin/deliveries.htmlFull on Delivery Mgmt; manage Drivers, Routes, Serviceable Areas and Delivery Settings; view Inventory and Reports.
Procurement Manager (procurement)Admin/admin/procurement.htmlFull on Procurement; manage Farmers; view Quality Testing and Reports.
Accountant (accountant)Admin/admin/revenue.htmlFull on Payments, Wallet, Expenses, Payroll and Salary Advances; manage Billing and Reports; view Revenue, Coupons and several HR screens.
Inventory Manager (inventory)Admin/admin/inventory.htmlFull on Inventory and Bottle Inventory; view Reports.
Quality Control (quality)Admin/admin/quality.htmlFull on Quality Testing; view Procurement and Reports.
Marketing Manager (marketing)Admin/admin/coupons.htmlFull on Coupons, Offers and Blogs; manage CMS, Notifications and Pure Rewards; view Reports.
Delivery Executive (delivery_executive)Delivery app/delivery/dashboard.htmlManage their Delivery Mgmt queue; view Routes. Never sees the admin console.
CustomerMy-Account/account/dashboard.htmlNo admin modules at all — only their own account. Cannot reach any /admin URL.
⚠️
The four "critical reserved" areas even Admin cannot fully touch

By design the ordinary Admin role is deliberately blocked from four sensitive areas so that no one but the Super Admin can quietly widen their own powers: Permissions (no access), and Settings, Roles and Audit Logs (view-only). This is baked into the default matrix and mirrored on the server. If you need someone to help manage roles, that person effectively needs to be a Super Admin — think hard before granting that.

ℹ️
"Surface" decides which app a role even sees

Before any module permission is checked, DOODLY checks the role's surface. Only admin-surface roles ever get the admin navigation or reach /admin/* pages. A Customer or Delivery Executive is denied every admin URL outright — no matter what the permission matrix says — and their admin navigation is empty. This is a hard rule in both the client (canRoute, filterNav) and the server (canAccessPath).

Tab 1 — Roles

The master list of roles: create new ones, duplicate an existing one, jump to its matrix, assign people, or delete a custom role.

Purpose Manage the set of roles itself — this is where new job roles are born and where you pick which role's matrix to edit.
Access Super Admin Path Admin → System → Roles & Permissions → Roles
Toolbar — Search roles… · New role name… box · Copy from dropdown · Create role button
Search boxType to filter the role list by name.
New role nameFree-text name for a brand-new role.
Copy-from select"Blank" or "Copy: <role>" — start the new role empty or from an existing role's permissions.
Roles tableRole (with Default / Custom / Full-access badge) · Assigned (user count) · Manage (action links).
Row action linksEdit matrix · Duplicate · Assign users · Delete (custom roles only).
Figure — the Roles tab

The roles table — columns explained

ColumnWhat it showsNotes
RoleThe role's display name plus a badge.Default = built-in; Custom = one you created; Full access = the Super Admin row.
AssignedHow many active users currently hold this role.Reads "0 users", "1 user", "5 users" etc. In live mode this count comes from the database.
ManageThe action links for that role.Super Admin shows only "All permissions" (nothing to edit). Default roles show Edit matrix / Duplicate / Assign users. Custom roles additionally show Delete.

Create-a-role form

FieldPurposeAccepted valuesReq.Validation / backendExample
New role nameHuman name for the role.Free text.YesMust not be empty ("Enter a role name"). Turned into a key like role_<slug>; if that key already exists you get "That role already exists". On the server the key is lower-cased, non-alphanumerics become underscores, and it must be at least 2 characters.Night Shift Supervisor
Copy fromSeed the new role's permissions from an existing role, instead of starting blank."Blank" (default) or any non-Super-Admin role.Opt.When set, the source role's whole level matrix is copied as a starting point (the client clones it; the server clones each module level into RolePermission).Copy: Operations Manager

Buttons & row actions on this tab

ButtonWhat it doesBackend processPermission
Create roleAdds a new role using the name and copy-from selection.Client createRole() makes a custom role; a write-through call posts {action:"create"} (or {action:"clone", cloneFrom}) to /api/roles, saving a RoleDef row in Postgres.Super Admin
Edit matrixJumps to the Role Matrix tab with this role pre-selected.Client only — switches the active tab and role.Super Admin
DuplicateInstantly makes a copy of the role (named "<role> Copy") with the same permissions.duplicateRole() → same as clone; posts {action:"clone"} to /api/roles.Super Admin
Assign usersOpens a modal to move people into this role (see below).assignUsersToRole() updates each selected user's role.Super Admin
DeleteRemoves a custom role after a confirmation. Any users on it are moved to Customer Support.Asks "Delete this custom role? Its users move to Customer Support." → deleteRole() + posts {action:"delete"}, removing the RoleDef and its permissions.Super Admin

Assign-users modal

Reached from the "Assign users" link on any role.

Modal — Assign users → <Role> · ✕ close
Checklist of all usersEvery non-deleted user, each with a checkbox and their current role in small grey text. People already in this role are pre-checked.
Save assignmentsMoves every ticked user into this role and shows "N user(s) assigned to <Role>".
Figure — the Assign users modal
⚠️
Important — custom roles are permission templates, not fully assignable job titles

In the live database a user's role is stored as a fixed list (an enum) of the eleven built-in roles. That means a brand-new custom role you invent here is a reusable permission template the platform reads, but it cannot be permanently written onto a user account on the backend the way a built-in role can. Treat custom roles as a way to design and preview a permission set; for a role people will actually be assigned to long-term, prefer duplicating and shaping one of the built-in roles. This limitation is documented in the server code.

Screen note shown under the table: "Default roles can be duplicated into editable custom roles. Deleting a custom role reassigns its users to Customer Support."

Tab 2 — Role Matrix

A big grid: one row per module, one column per action. Switch any single action on or off for the whole selected role.

Purpose Tune permissions finer than the broad level — flip one exact action (e.g. "Delete" on Reports) for everyone in a role.
Access Super Admin Path Admin → System → Roles & Permissions → Role Matrix
Toolbar — Role selector · Search modules… · Group filter · Reset role grants button
Role selectorChoose which role you are editing. Pick Super Admin and the grid is replaced by a note — it can't be edited.
Module searchFilter the rows by module name or group.
Group filterAll groups, or one group (Overview, Commerce, Finance, Operations, Supply, Catalogue, Inventory, Growth, Marketing, Content, System, People, HR).
The matrixRows = modules. Columns = View · Create · Edit · Delete · Export · Approve · Print, plus a Special column.
Toggle cellsEach cell is an on/off switch. Amber = explicitly set (overrides the level default).
Reset role grantsClears every explicit toggle for this role, dropping it back to its plain level defaults.
Figure — the Role Matrix tab

Reading a matrix cell

AppearanceMeaningHow it got there
Switch ON (filled)The role is allowed this action on this module.Either the role's level already includes it, or you explicitly turned it on.
Switch OFF (empty)The role is not allowed this action.The level doesn't cover it, or you explicitly turned it off.
Amber highlightThis action has been explicitly overridden for the role, beating the level default.You clicked it — it now stores a hard "allow" or "deny" (setRoleAction) rather than inheriting from the level.
– greyed cellThat action does not apply to this module.The action isn't in the module's applicable set (for example "Print" on a background job).
Special columnHolds the module's extra actions when it has any.Examples: Payments has Refund; Wallet has Reverse; Inventory has Adjust; Deliveries has Assign; GST has Manage Settings.

The seven core columns

ColumnGrants the ability to…
ViewOpen and read the module.
CreateAdd new records in it.
EditChange existing records.
DeleteRemove records (usually a soft-delete elsewhere in DOODLY).
ExportDownload the data (CSV / Excel / PDF).
ApproveSign off items that need approval (e.g. expenses, billing, referrals).
PrintProduce a printable copy (e.g. invoices).
ℹ️
How "level" and "explicit toggle" fit together

A role's baseline is its level per module (None / View / Manage / Full). View = just View. Manage = View + Create + Edit + Export. Full = all of those + Delete, plus that module's specials. On top of that baseline, a toggle you flip here writes an explicit grant or deny for one action that the system checks first. So you can, for instance, keep a role at "Manage" on Reports but explicitly add just the single "Delete" action — without promoting the whole module to Full. The amber highlight is your reminder that a cell is no longer simply inheriting.

Buttons on this tab

ButtonWhat it doesBackend processPermission
Toggle cellFlips one action on/off for the whole role.setRoleAction(role, module, action, on) stores an explicit allow/deny and writes an audit entry. Enforced in the UI immediately; mirrored server-side in production.Super Admin
Reset role grantsWipes all explicit toggles for the selected role, reverting to its level defaults.Clears this role's entries from the granular-grant store and logs "reset explicit grants for <role>".Super Admin

Screen note: "Toggling a cell sets an explicit grant/deny for <role> that overrides the role's level defaults. Amber = explicitly set." Choosing Super Admin in the selector shows only: "Super Admin has every permission on every module — not editable."

Tab 3 — User Permissions

Give one specific person an exception — grant them something their role lacks, or revoke something their role grants.

Purpose Handle the "this one person is special" cases without inventing a whole new role.
Access Super Admin Path Admin → System → Roles & Permissions → User Permissions
Toolbar — Search users… · Group filter · (then a two-panel layout below)
User list (left)Every person as a row: avatar initial, name, email · role, and a badge — "N override(s)" (amber) or "inherits role" (grey).
Selected-user header (right)Their name, email and role, plus two counters: effective perms and overrides.
Preview as user👁 button — opens the platform exactly as this person sees it (their nav, routes and buttons).
Reset overridesAppears only when the person has overrides; wipes them all back to pure role inheritance.
LegendInherit (role default) · ✓ Granted (override) · ✗ Revoked (override).
Permission gridSame module × action layout as the Role Matrix, but each cell cycles Inherit → Grant → Revoke.
Figure — the User Permissions tab

The three states of a user cell — click to cycle

StateSymbolMeaning
Inherit (default)✓ or ✗ with no dotFollows whatever the person's role allows — no personal exception. The ✓/✗ simply shows the role's answer.
Grant (override)✓ with an override dotThis person is allowed the action even if their role isn't.
Revoke (override)✗ with an override dotThis person is denied the action even if their role grants it.
✅
The precedence rule — a user override always wins

When DOODLY works out whether a person may do something, it checks: is there a user Grant? → yes, allow. A user Revoke? → yes, deny. Otherwise fall back to the role default (which itself already factors in any amber role toggle). That is why a single revoke on one person is the cleanest way to take one capability away from one employee without touching their whole team. The counters at the top-right — effective perms and overrides — update live so you can see the effect of what you just did.

Buttons on this tab

ButtonWhat it doesBackend processPermission
Cell clickCycles that action for this person: Inherit → Grant → Revoke → Inherit.setUserPerm(userId, module, action, state) records the override and writes an audit line "old → new".Super Admin
👁 Preview as userLoads the whole platform as this person — their exact navigation, allowed routes and visible buttons.viewAsUser() starts a Super-Admin "view as" session and redirects to the person's role home page. Return to yourself from the impersonation banner.Super Admin
Reset overridesRemoves every personal exception for the selected user.Confirms "Remove all permission overrides for this user…" → clearUserPerms() and audits the clear.Super Admin
⚠️
Prefer roles over piles of personal overrides

Individual overrides are perfect for a genuine one-off, but if you find yourself giving five people the same three grants, that is a sign you should make (or duplicate) a role instead. Scattered per-person exceptions are hard to audit and easy to forget. Keep the "overrides" counter low for each person wherever you can.

Tab 4 — Audit

A focused history of every role and permission change made from this screen.

Purpose Answer "who changed this permission, and from what to what?" without leaving the page.
Access Super Admin Path Admin → System → Roles & Permissions → Audit
Panel — Permission & role change history (most recent 60 events)
WhoThe Super Admin who made the change.
ActionA short code — e.g. role.create, role.assign, permission.role, permission.user, permission.change.
Detail (old → new)What changed, including the previous and new value.
WhenLocal date & time (India).
Figure — the Audit tab
ℹ️
This is a filtered view of the wider audit trail

This tab shows only role, user and permission events (the most recent 60). The full platform log — logins, role switches, and every other sensitive action, with device, browser and IP — lives on the dedicated Admin → System → Audit Logs screen. When it's empty you'll see: "No permission/role/user events yet — make a change to generate an entry."

What generates an audit entry

Action codeWritten when you…
role.createCreate a role (notes if it was duplicated from another).
role.deleteDelete a custom role.
role.assignAssign users into a role via the modal.
role.switchSwitch roles or "Preview as user".
permission.roleToggle a single action for a role (or reset a role's grants).
permission.userGrant / revoke / clear a person's individual override.
permission.changeChange a role's module level on the Permissions page.

The "Run tests" self-check

A safe, one-click diagnostic that proves the access-control engine is behaving.

Purpose Let a Super Admin confirm — without any risk to real data — that inheritance, overrides and precedence all still work.
Where The Run tests button sits at the top-right of the tab bar; results appear next to it as a green (all passed) or red (some failed) badge.
💡
It never touches your live settings

When you click Run tests, the engine takes a snapshot of the relevant saved data, runs twelve checks on a copy, then restores everything exactly as it was. You'll see something like 12/12 tests passed; hover the badge to read each check by name. Use it any time you doubt a permission is applying correctly.

The twelve checks, in plain words

#What it verifies
1With no override, a user simply inherits their role's permission.
2A Grant override adds a permission the role does not have.
3A Revoke override removes a permission the role does have.
4A user override takes precedence over the role default.
5Setting a cell back to Inherit reverts to the role default.
6The "overrides" counter matches the number of active overrides.
7A granular role toggle enables an action for the whole role.
8A granular role toggle can also disable an action.
9Duplicating a role copies its permissions faithfully.
10Permission changes are written to the audit log.
11The "effective perms" count updates when an override is added.
12Applying the same override twice is safe (idempotent).

How the settings are actually enforced

Where the rules live, and why a bad edit can't lock everyone out.

Purpose Understand that this screen is configuration on top of a hard-coded safety net.
Workflow — how one permission decision is made
Is the role Super Admin? → allow allUser Grant / Revoke override?Explicit role toggle (amber)?Role's module level (None/View/Manage/Full)Allow or Deny
Figure — the order in which DOODLY resolves "can this person do X?"
LayerWhere it livesRole in the system
Code default matrixnext-app/lib/rbac.ts (server) & assets/js/rbac.js (client)The hard, edge-safe security boundary. Never database-dependent, so a bad override can never lock people out at the security layer.
Saved role overrides & custom rolesPostgres tables RoleDef and RolePermission, via /api/rolesDurable, auditable configuration layered on top of the code default. Create / clone / delete on the Roles tab and module levels on the Permissions page save here.
Granular role toggles & user overridesEnforced in the UI (this build) and mirrored server-side in productionThe finest layers — a single action for a whole role, or for one person.
API permission gateEvery backend route calls requirePermission(module, action)e.g. viewing this data needs roles:view; creating / cloning / deleting a role needs roles:edit, which only the Super Admin holds. A denied call returns HTTP 403.
✅
Best Practice — a safe change routine

1) Make one change. 2) Open User Permissions and use 👁 Preview as user to see the platform through an affected person's eyes. 3) Check the Audit tab shows your change with the right old → new value. 4) If in doubt, click Run tests. 5) Return to yourself. Small, verified steps beat a sweeping edit you can't easily undo.

Troubleshooting

Common situations and how to resolve them.

SymptomLikely causeFix
The whole screen shows a padlock and "Roles & Permissions is restricted".You are not signed in as the Super Admin — every other role gets read-only.Sign in as the Super Admin. This module is intentionally Super-Admin-only.
Banner reads "⚠ Roles are Super Admin only (403)."The backend rejected the request because your role lacks roles:edit.Only the Super Admin can create/clone/delete roles. Use a Super Admin account.
"That role already exists" when creating a role.A role with the same generated key already exists (built-in or custom).Choose a different name, or duplicate/edit the existing role instead.
A person still can't do something after you granted it.A user Revoke override, or an amber role deny, is beating the grant; or their role's surface isn't admin.Open User Permissions, check for a Revoke on that action, and confirm the role is an admin-surface role. Use Preview as user to verify.
Deleting a custom role — where did its people go?By design, deleting a custom role reassigns its users to Customer Support.Reassign them to the correct role from the Roles tab (Assign users) or from User Management.
An Admin says they can't open Permissions / edit Settings / Roles / Audit.Those four are "critical reserved" — Admin gets none/view only.This is intended. Only the Super Admin manages those areas.
A custom role you made isn't sticking on a user account.User roles are a fixed enum on the backend; custom keys are templates, not permanently assignable.Duplicate and shape a built-in role for long-term assignment; use custom roles for designing permission sets.

Frequently asked questions

Quick answers for new administrators.

What is the difference between the "Permissions" page and this "Roles & Permissions" page?

The Permissions page is a simple grid for setting each role's broad level per module (None / View / Manage / Full) and saves those levels to the database. This page is the advanced control: it manages the roles themselves, lets you toggle a single action for a role, and lets you grant or revoke one action for one person. Use Permissions for the big strokes and this page for the fine detail.

If I change a role's permission, does it affect people already in that role?

Yes — a role is a shared bundle. Every person in the role immediately gets the new set of permissions (minus any personal overrides they have). That is exactly why roles are efficient, and why you should change them thoughtfully.

Can I make a role that can do everything except manage roles?

The built-in Admin role is already close to that — full access to nearly everything, but held out of Permissions, Roles, Settings-edit and Audit-Logs-edit. Those four areas are deliberately reserved for the Super Admin, so a role that manages roles is effectively a Super Admin.

How do I temporarily see what a delivery executive or accountant sees?

Go to User Permissions, pick that person, and click 👁 Preview as user. You'll be dropped into the platform with their exact navigation, allowed pages and buttons. Use the banner to return to yourself when you're done.

Is every change here recorded?

Yes. Every role creation, deletion, assignment, toggle and override is written to the audit trail with who, when and the old → new value — visible on the Audit tab here and in full on the Audit Logs screen.

DOODLYAdmin · System · Audit Logs
§Admin · System

Audit Logs

An append-only, tamper-evident record of every sensitive action across DOODLY — who did what, when, from where and how — with change tracking (old → new value), a live dashboard, powerful search and filters, a per-user activity timeline, sensitive-activity alerts, CSV / Excel / PDF export and configurable retention.

ℹ️
Note — what the Audit Log is (and is not)

The Audit Log is the platform's black box recorder. It captures a rich record for every meaningful event — logins and failed logins, user and role changes, permission grants, order status changes, payments, price and GST edits, report exports, deliveries, procurement and more. Records are append-only: they can never be edited or deleted through the application. There is deliberately no "delete" and no "edit" button anywhere on this screen — the only way records leave the active log is retention/archive, and even archived records can be restored. This is what makes the log trustworthy for investigations and compliance.

💡
How the records get created

You never write audit records by hand. Every module in DOODLY calls one central logger (DOODLY_RBAC.audit() in the browser, lib/auth/audit.ts on the server), which auto-classifies each action into a Module · Action · Entity, extracts any old → new change out of the description, stamps the actor, role, department, IP, browser, OS, device and session, and appends it. In production this screen reads the real Postgres AuditLog table (up to the most recent 2,000 records) through /api/admin/audit-logs and mirrors it into the viewer. A live green banner at the top confirms the source and record count; opening the screen is itself audited as an audit.view event.

⚠️
Who can open this screen

Audit Logs is a System module reserved for the highest trust levels. By default Super Admin has full access and Admin has view-only access; every other role is denied and does not even see the menu item. Exporting the log (CSV / Excel / PDF) and the Retention tab are Super Admin only — an Admin can read and search but cannot export or change the retention policy. The backend enforces the same rule and returns 403 Forbidden to anyone without the auditLogs · view permission.

Screen 1 — Audit Dashboard

Today's activity health at a glance, plus sensitive-activity alerts and a breakdown of where the activity is happening.

Purpose Answer "is anything unusual happening on the platform today?" in one look, then click any number to drill straight into the matching events.
Access Super Admin full · Admin view-only Path Admin → System → Audit Logs → Dashboard
Audit Logs — Dashboard · tabs: Dashboard · Activity Logs · User Timeline · Retention (Super Admin) · Run tests
Live source banner"● Live — N audit record(s) from the DOODLY database … Append-only."
Stat cards (7)Total Activities Today · Total Logins · Failed Logins · Data Modifications · Reports Downloaded · Orders Edited · System Changes — each clickable
Sensitive activity alertsFailed-login bursts, permission / role changes, deletions, config changes, high export volume (last 24h)
Activity by moduleEvery module with its total event count, most-active first, with an icon
Figure 1 — Dashboard tab: a seven-card stat strip above the alerts panel and the module-activity panel.

Stat cards

All seven cards count today's events only, and every card is a button — clicking it jumps to the Activity Logs tab pre-filtered to those events.

Stat cardWhat it counts (today)Click jumps to…
Total Activities TodayEvery audit record stamped with today's dateLogs, cleared filters
Total LoginsSuccessful sign-ins (auth.login)Logs filtered Action = Login
Failed LoginsFailed sign-ins (auth.login_failed) — the card turns amber when above zeroLogs filtered Action = Failed Login
Data ModificationsCreate / update / edit / delete / change / status / adjust / set / reset events (excluding Authentication and Reports)Logs filtered Status = Success
Reports DownloadedEvents in the Reports module (exports, downloads, prints)Logs filtered Module = Reports
Orders EditedOrders-module events that edit / update / change status / cancelLogs filtered Module = Orders
System ChangesBusiness Settings events (GST, pricing, referral, settings, invoice config)Logs filtered Module = Business Settings

Sensitive activity alerts

This panel scans the last 24 hours and raises a coloured alert whenever a risky pattern appears. Each alert is clickable and drops you into the Activity Logs pre-searched for that pattern. When nothing is flagged it reads "No sensitive activity in the last 24 hours."

AlertTrigger (rolling 24h)ToneMeaning
Failed login attemptsA single user reaches 3 or more failed loginsRedPossible brute-force / account takeover attempt
Permission change(s)Any permission.* eventAmberSomeone's access rights were granted or revoked — review
Role change(s)Any role.* eventAmberRoles assigned or modified
Deletion(s)Any user.delete or *.delete eventRedRecords were deleted in the last 24 hours
System configuration change(s)Any Business Settings eventAmberGST, pricing, settings or referral configuration changed
Exports / downloads5 or more Reports or audit.export eventsBlueUnusually high data-export volume — check for data exfiltration
✅
Best Practice — start your day on this tab

Open the Dashboard first thing each morning. If the Failed Logins card is amber or a red alert is showing, click straight through and investigate before doing anything else. A 30-second daily glance catches most access problems while they are still small.

Screen 2 — Activity Logs

The full searchable, filterable, paginated table of every audit record, with export and a click-through detail card.

Purpose Find and inspect any individual event — trace who changed a price, when a customer record was edited, which device a login came from — and export the result for evidence.
Access Super Admin view + export · Admin view only (Export buttons are blocked by the export permission) Path Admin → System → Audit Logs → Activity Logs
Activity Logs — search + filters + table · CSV · Excel · PDF
Search boxFree text over user, action, module, description, entity, old/new value and audit ID
Filter row (8)User · Role · Module · Action · Status · Entity Type · Entity ID · From / To dates
Count + Clear + Export"N events" · Clear filters · CSV / Excel / PDF
Data table (8 columns)When · User · Module · Action · Entity · Change · Status · Device / IP
Pager25 / 50 / 100 rows per page · ‹ Prev / Next › · "Page X of Y"
Row click → detail cardOpens the full-record modal (see Screen 3)
Figure 2 — Activity Logs tab: a keyword box, an eight-field filter row, an eight-column table and a pager with export buttons.

Filters — field reference

FieldPurposeAccepted valuesReq.Validation / backendExample
SearchFree-text keyword match across the recordAny textOpt.Case-insensitive; matches user, action, module, description, entity type/ID, old value, new value and audit ID; re-filters as you typeC-4821
UserRestrict to one actorAny user seen in the logOpt.Dropdown auto-built from users present in the log, most-active firstAarav Sharma
RoleRestrict to one roleSuper Admin, Admin, Accountant…Opt.Options come from the RBAC role list; matches the record's stored role keyAccountant
ModuleRestrict to one functional areaAuthentication, User Management, Orders, Payments, Reports, Business Settings…Opt.Built from the modules present in the logPayments
ActionRestrict to one action labelLogin, Failed Login, User Created, Order Status Changed…Opt.Distinct action labels found in the log, alphabeticalFailed Login
StatusOutcome of the actionSuccess · FailedOpt.Failed is set automatically for *_failed / *.fail actionsFailed
Entity TypeRestrict to one kind of objectSession, User, Customer, Order, Subscription, Payment, Report, Stock, Purchase, Delivery, Setting, Business, Expense, AuditOpt.Fixed list of entity types the classifier assignsOrder
Entity IDFind events touching one specific recordAny partial IDOpt.Case-insensitive "contains" match on the record's entity IDC-4821
FromStart of the date rangeDateOpt.Keeps events on or after this date (compares the record's date part)2026-07-01
ToEnd of the date rangeDateOpt.Keeps events on or before this date2026-07-12

Table specification

PropertyDetail
Columns (8)When (timestamp + audit ID beneath, e.g. AUD-00000123) · User (name + role beneath) · Module (icon + name) · Action · Entity (type + entity ID beneath) · Change (old → new, or "—") · Status (green Success / red Failed badge) · Device / IP (device + IP beneath)
SearchSingle box; case-insensitive across user, action, module, description, entity type, entity ID, old/new value and audit ID
FiltersEight: User · Role · Module · Action · Status · Entity Type · Entity ID · From / To (all combine with AND)
SortingFixed — newest event first (append-only order); columns are not manually re-sortable
Pagination25 rows per page by default; page-size selector offers 25 / 50 / 100; ‹ Prev / Next › with "Page X of Y"
ExportsCSV, Excel (.xls) and PDF (print dialog) — export the currently filtered rows, not just the visible page (Super Admin only)
Row actionsClick any row to open the full-record detail card (Screen 3). There are no edit / delete row actions by design; an empty result shows "No matching audit events."

Buttons on this screen

ButtonWhat it doesBackend processPermission
Clear filtersResets the keyword box and all eight filters, returns to page 1Client-side state reset; re-renders the full listSuper AdminAdmin
‹ Prev / Next ›Moves one page back or forward through the filtered resultsClient-side paging over the filtered setSuper AdminAdmin
Rows-per-page (25 / 50 / 100)Changes how many events show per pageClient-side; resets to page 1Super AdminAdmin
CSVDownloads the filtered rows as audit-log.csv (18 columns)Builds CSV in the browser; posts an audit.export event so the export is itself loggedSuper Admin
ExcelDownloads the filtered rows as audit-log.xlsBuilds an Excel-compatible HTML table; logs audit.exportSuper Admin
PDFOpens the browser print dialog to save the log as PDFNative print; logs audit.exportSuper Admin
Run testsRuns the built-in self-check suite and shows an "X/Y tests" badge (green when all pass)In-memory unit tests of classify / change-tracking / filtering / append-only / performance; snapshots and restores storage — writes nothing permanentSuper AdminAdmin
⛔
Caution — exports are sensitive and are themselves audited

The moment you press CSV, Excel or PDF, DOODLY writes an audit.export record capturing who exported how many records in which format — so the act of taking data out of the platform is permanently on the record. Treat every export as sensitive customer/business data: do not email it, and delete local copies once the investigation is closed. Repeated exports also raise the blue "high export volume" alert on the Dashboard.

Screen 3 — Full-Record Detail Card

The complete who / what / when / where / how of a single audit event, opened by clicking any row.

Purpose Show every captured field for one event in a clean read-only card so you can verify exactly what happened, from which device and session.
Access Super Admin · Admin (read-only — no edit controls exist) Path Admin → System → Audit Logs → Activity Logs → (click a row)
Detail card — Action + Status · Audit ID · timestamp · ✕ close
WhoUser · Role · Department
WhatModule · Action · Action key · Entity type · Entity ID
ChangeOld value → New value (highlighted, only when present)
Where / HowIP address · Browser · Operating system · Device · Session ID · Location
ContextDescription · Status
Close✕ top-right, "Close" button, click-outside or Esc key
Figure 3 — Full-record modal: a read-only breakdown of one event across who / what / change / where-how.

Every field on the detail card

FieldWhat it tells youExample
UserThe person (or "System") who performed the actionRohan Mehta
RoleThe role they were acting as at the timeAccountant
DepartmentDepartment derived from the roleFinance
ModuleFunctional area the action belongs toPayments
ActionHuman-readable action labelPayment Received
Action keyThe raw machine key that generated the recordb2b.order.pay
Entity typeThe kind of object the action touchedPayment
Entity IDThe specific record's identifierB2B-ORD-2026-000004
ChangeOld value → New value, shown only when the event changed something₹78 → ₹76
DescriptionFree-text summary of what happenedDairy 5%→12%
IP addressNetwork address the action came from192.168.24.15
BrowserBrowser usedChrome
Operating systemOS of the deviceWindows 10/11
DeviceDevice classDesktop
Session IDThe login session the action belonged toS-1A2B3C4D
LocationGeo location if resolved (else "—")—
StatusOutcome badge — green Success / red FailedSuccess
ℹ️
Note — the card is strictly read-only

There is no "Edit", "Correct" or "Delete" control on the detail card — only Close. That is intentional: if a record looks wrong, you do not fix it, you add a new corrective event elsewhere in the system. The original stays exactly as captured. Blank fields (like Location) are simply hidden rather than shown empty.

Screen 4 — User Timeline

One person's entire activity history, grouped by day, with a quick behaviour summary.

Purpose Reconstruct exactly what a single staff member did and when — essential for onboarding reviews, incident investigations and off-boarding checks.
Access Super Admin view + export · Admin view only Path Admin → System → Audit Logs → User Timeline
User Timeline — user picker · summary chips · Export timeline
User selectorDropdown of everyone who appears in the log
Summary chips (5)Logins · Logouts · Reports · Orders · Permission changes for the chosen user
Export timelineDownloads this user's full activity as CSV (Super Admin)
Day groupsEvents grouped under each day header (e.g. "Mon, 12 Jul 2026")
Event rowsIcon · action · module · description · old → new · time · status — click to open the detail card
Figure 4 — User Timeline tab: pick a user, read their five behaviour chips, then scroll their day-by-day event stream.

Choose a user and the screen rebuilds their entire history newest-day first. The five summary chips count that user's Logins, Logouts, Reports touched, Orders touched and Permission changes (role or permission events). Each event row shows the module icon, the action, the description and any old → new change, with the time and a Success/Failed badge on the right; clicking a row opens the same full-record detail card from Screen 3. If the user has no activity the screen reads "No activity for this user."

ElementDetail
User selectorLists every distinct user found in the log; defaults to the most-active user
GroupingEvents are bucketed by calendar day, days shown newest first, events within a day newest first
Summary chipsLogins · Logouts · Reports · Orders · Permission changes — computed live for the selected user
Row actionClick any event to open its full-record detail card
Export timelineCSV of the selected user's complete timeline; itself logged as an audit.export event (Super Admin only)
✅
Best Practice — off-boarding checklist

Before you disable a departing employee's account, open their User Timeline and export it. This gives you a permanent, dated record of everything they did in their final weeks — invaluable if a question comes up months later. Pair it with the User Management "disable" step so access ends and the history is preserved in one sitting.

Screen 5 — Retention & Archive

Control how long records stay in the active log and archive or restore older records. Super Admin only.

Purpose Keep the active log fast and manageable while never losing history — old records move to a restorable archive rather than being deleted.
Access Super Admin only — this tab is hidden from every other role Path Admin → System → Audit Logs → Retention
Retention & archive — policy form + archive actions
Retention period (days)How long records stay in the active log (default 365)
Max active recordsCap on the active log; overflow auto-archives (default 5,000)
Save policyPersists the two settings; logs a settings-update event
Archive old nowImmediately moves records older than the retention window to the archive
Restore archive (N)Pulls every archived record back into the active log
Counters"Active records: N · Archived: N"
Figure 5 — Retention tab: two policy fields, three action buttons and live active/archived counters.

Policy fields

FieldPurposeAccepted valuesReq.Validation / backendExample
Retention period (days)How many days a record stays in the active log before it is eligible for archiveWhole number of daysYesFalls back to 365 if left blank; applied on load and whenever "Archive old now" runs365
Max active recordsHard cap on active-log size; anything beyond the cap overflows into the archive automatically as new records arriveWhole numberYesFalls back to 5,000 if left blank5000

Buttons

ButtonWhat it doesBackend processPermission
Save policySaves the retention period and max-records valuesPersists the settings and writes a settings.update audit event ("Audit retention policy updated")Super Admin
Archive old nowMoves records older than the retention window out of the active log immediatelyRuns the retention sweep; shows how many records were archived (or "Nothing to archive")Super Admin
Restore archive (N)Brings all archived records back into the active log and re-sorts newest-firstMerges the archive into the active log and empties the archive; the count N shows how many are currently archivedSuper Admin
⚠️
Archiving is not deleting

"Archive old now" and the automatic overflow never destroy data — records move to a separate archive that "Restore archive" can pull back at any time. There is deliberately no way to permanently erase audit records from this screen. Retention only keeps the active log fast; for long-term compliance the manual also notes the design is future-ready for SIEM streaming and compliance reporting off the same append-only writer.

ℹ️
Where retention runs automatically

Retention is also applied every time the Audit Logs screen is opened, so the active log is trimmed to your policy without anyone pressing a button. The two policy numbers therefore quietly govern the whole log's size in the background.

Workflow — from action to searchable audit record
User performs an action anywhere in DOODLYCentral logger is called (audit key + description)Auto-classify → Module · Action · Entity · SensitivityExtract old → new change + stamp who / when / IP / device / sessionAppend (never edit/delete); dedupe identical events within 1.5sServer writes the Postgres AuditLog rowScreen reads & mirrors it, live, into Dashboard / Logs / Timeline
Figure 6 — Every meaningful action is auto-classified, enriched and appended; the viewer only ever reads.
ℹ️
Note — the data behind the screen

In production each row comes from the Postgres AuditLog table (fields: user, role, action, target, IP, device, browser, timestamp), joined to the user's name for display and served read-only from /api/admin/audit-logs (views: list, facets, logins). A companion LoginHistory table records every sign-in success/failure and feeds the failed-login alerts. The API is guarded by the same auditLogs · view permission as the screen and returns 403 to anyone else.

Troubleshooting

SymptomLikely causeFix
"Access restricted — you don't have permission to view audit logs"Your role lacks the auditLogs · view permission (only Super Admin and, by default, Admin have it)Ask a Super Admin to grant view access, or use an account that has it
Banner reads "⚠ Audit Logs are Super Admin only (403)"The backend rejected the read because the signed-in role is not permittedSign in as Super Admin; the 403 comes from the API's permission check, not a bug
Banner reads "⚠ Backend offline — start next-app"The Next.js API is not running, so the live database records can't be loadedStart the backend (next-app); the screen still shows any locally cached records meanwhile
CSV / Excel / PDF buttons do nothing / toast "You don't have permission to export"You are signed in as Admin (view-only); export needs the export permissionExport as Super Admin, or ask one to run the export
Expected event isn't in the logAn active filter or date range is hiding it, or it happened before your retention windowClick "Clear filters"; widen the From/To dates; if older than retention, use "Restore archive"
Two identical events seem to be missing / mergedThe writer de-duplicates the same action + description by the same user within 1.5 secondsThis is intentional double-click protection; genuinely distinct events are always kept
Record shows the actor as "System" with role "system"The action had no signed-in user (a background/server task), or the actor's name couldn't be resolvedNormal for automated events; check the IP, session and description for context

Frequently asked questions

Can anyone edit or delete an audit record to cover their tracks?

No. The log is append-only by design — there is no edit or delete control anywhere in the UI or the API, and even the built-in self-test verifies that no such function is exposed. Records can only be archived (and restored), never erased. That is exactly what makes the log trustworthy as evidence.

What is the difference between "archive" and "delete"?

Archiving moves older records out of the fast active log into a separate store to keep things responsive; "Restore archive" brings them all back. Nothing is destroyed. There is no delete operation for audit data at all.

Why can an Admin see the log but not export it?

Reading the log is low-risk; taking a copy of it out of the platform is sensitive. By default Admin gets the view permission but not export, so only a Super Admin can produce CSV/Excel/PDF files — and every such export is itself recorded as an audit.export event.

Does opening this screen create a record?

Yes. Simply opening Audit Logs writes an audit.view event ("Opened audit logs"), and every export writes an audit.export event. The audit trail even audits the people auditing it.

How far back does the log go?

The active log holds up to your "Max active records" cap (default 5,000) and records within your retention window (default 365 days); the live screen loads up to the most recent 2,000 from the database. Older records sit in the archive and can be restored when you need to investigate further back.

✅
Best Practice — use the log, don't just keep it

Make the Audit Log part of your weekly rhythm, not a box you open only after an incident. A five-minute weekly pass — glance at the Dashboard alerts, spot-check any permission or role changes, and skim exports — turns the log from passive insurance into active early warning. When something does go wrong, you will already know the log inside out.

DOODLYAdmin · System · Settings
§Admin · System

Settings

The single place to set DOODLY's brand identity, notification channels and login-security policy — saved to the DOODLY database and applied wherever the platform reads them.

ℹ️
Note

Settings is a general-purpose configuration store for the handful of platform values that have no dedicated screen of their own. It is organised into three groups — General, Notifications and Security. Everything else (delivery timings, pincodes, GST rates, referral rules, roles) lives on its own page and is only linked from here, never edited here.

Settings

One long form of three configuration panels, a “managed elsewhere” link list, and a single Save action.

Purpose Hold platform-wide configuration — brand name, support contacts, currency/timezone/date format, GSTIN, notification-channel switches and the login-security policy — in one auditable place.
Access View: Super Admin Admin · Edit & Save: Super Admin only (Admin sees the same form, read-only). No other role can open this screen. Path Admin → System → Settings
Data source Live from GET /api/admin/app-settings; saves go to POST /api/admin/app-settings. General/notify/security keys are stored in the AppSetting key/value table; Legal company name and GSTIN are routed to the shared BillingConfig record so invoices stay consistent.
Header — Settings · “Platform configuration — general, notifications and security” (no header buttons)
Live status bannerGreen “● Live — platform settings from the DOODLY database” with the API base URL, plus “Edits persist to Postgres + are audited” (Super Admin) or “View-only for your role” (Admin). Turns red if the backend is offline or your role is forbidden.
Panel — GeneralBrand name, Legal company name, Support phone, Support email, Business hours, GSTIN, Currency, Time zone, Date format (nine text fields laid out two/three per row).
Panel — NotificationsFour platform-wide channel toggles: Email, SMS, Push, WhatsApp. Helper line reminds you sending still needs provider credentials.
Panel — SecurityPassword min length, Session timeout (min), Max login attempts (three number fields) + “Require two-factor authentication (future-ready)” toggle.
Panel — Managed on their own pagesFive read-only links out to Delivery, Serviceable areas, GST rates, Referral & rewards and Roles & permissions.
Save barRight-aligned Save settings button (Super Admin). For everyone else this becomes an amber “Read-only” badge instead.
Figure — labelled layout of the Settings screen (top-to-bottom: banner, General, Notifications, Security, Managed-elsewhere, Save).

General

Brand identity, contact details and formatting defaults. All nine fields are free-text. Two of them — Legal company name and GSTIN — are read from and written back to the shared BillingConfig record, so a change here is the same value your business invoices print.

FieldPurposeAccepted valuesReq.Validation / backendExample
Brand namePublic-facing brand shown across the app.TextOpt.Key general.brandName; trimmed; stored in AppSetting. Default DOODLY.DOODLY
Legal company nameRegistered entity name used on invoices & statements.TextOpt.Routed to BillingConfig.companyName (not AppSetting). Blank input is ignored — the existing name is kept.Doodly Foods Pvt Ltd
Support phoneCustomer-care phone number.Text (phone)Opt.Key general.supportPhone; trimmed. Default +91 90000 00000.+91 90000 00000
Support emailCustomer-care e-mail address.Text (email)Opt.Key general.supportEmail; trimmed. Default care@doodly.app.care@doodly.app
Business hoursOperating hours shown to customers.TextOpt.Key general.businessHours; trimmed. Default 6:00 AM – 9:00 PM.6:00 AM – 9:00 PM
GSTINCompany GST identification number for tax documents.Text (15-char GSTIN)Opt.Routed to BillingConfig.gstin. Empty input clears it (stored as null). Placeholder shows the format.36ABCDE1234F1Z5
CurrencyDisplay currency code for money values.Text (ISO code)Opt.Key general.currency; trimmed. Default INR.INR
Time zonePlatform time zone for schedules & timestamps.Text (IANA zone)Opt.Key general.timezone; trimmed. Default Asia/Kolkata.Asia/Kolkata
Date formatPreferred date display pattern.Text (pattern)Opt.Key general.dateFormat; trimmed. Default DD/MM/YYYY.DD/MM/YYYY
⚠️
Warning

Currency, Time zone and Date format are stored as free text — the backend does not validate them against a list. Type an exact, standard value (for example INR, Asia/Kolkata, DD/MM/YYYY). A typo is saved as-is and could show wrong labels elsewhere.

Notifications

Four platform-wide switches that turn each messaging channel on or off. The panel carries a standing reminder: “Enable channels platform-wide. Actual sending still needs provider credentials (see remaining config).” — i.e. flipping a toggle on here does not by itself send anything; SMS/WhatsApp/Email/Push still require the provider keys to be configured.

FieldPurposeAccepted valuesReq.Validation / backendExample
Email notificationsMaster switch for the e-mail channel.On / Off (checkbox)Opt.Key notify.email; coerced to boolean. Default On.On
SMS notificationsMaster switch for the SMS channel.On / Off (checkbox)Opt.Key notify.sms; coerced to boolean. Default Off.Off
Push notificationsMaster switch for push notifications.On / Off (checkbox)Opt.Key notify.push; coerced to boolean. Default Off.Off
WhatsApp notificationsMaster switch for the WhatsApp channel.On / Off (checkbox)Opt.Key notify.whatsapp; coerced to boolean. Default Off.Off

Security

The login-security policy — password strength floor, how long an idle session survives, how many failed logins are allowed, and a future-ready two-factor flag.

FieldPurposeAccepted valuesReq.Validation / backendExample
Password min lengthMinimum characters a password must have.Whole number ≥ 0Opt.Key security.passwordMinLength. Field is type=number, min=0; server rounds and clamps to ≥ 0. Default 8.8
Session timeout (min)Idle minutes before a session expires.Whole number ≥ 0Opt.Key security.sessionTimeoutMin; rounded, clamped ≥ 0. Default 30.30
Max login attemptsFailed logins allowed before lock-out.Whole number ≥ 0Opt.Key security.maxLoginAttempts; rounded, clamped ≥ 0. Default 5.5
Require two-factor authenticationEnforce a second login factor (labelled “future-ready”).On / Off (checkbox)Opt.Key security.require2FA; boolean. Default Off. Reserved for a future 2FA rollout.Off
ℹ️
Note

Every field defaults to a sensible value even before anyone saves, because the backend seeds defaults (brand DOODLY, email on, password length 8, session 30 min, 5 attempts). A field only stops using its default once you save an explicit value over it.

Managed on their own pages

The bottom panel is a set of five read-only shortcuts. These settings deliberately do not live in this form — they each have a richer dedicated screen — so Settings simply points you to them.

LinkWhat it configuresWhere it goes
Delivery (cut-off, slots, auto-assign)Order cut-off time, delivery window and auto-assignment.Admin → Catalogue → Delivery Settings
Serviceable areas / pincodesWhich pincodes DOODLY delivers to, plus the waitlist.Admin → Operations → Serviceable Areas
